articles.title: Deadwood as Stage, Beetles as Evidence: Translating Forest Degradation into a Web-Based Narrative in Crete
articles.authors: JIAXI WANG
articles.abstract: Abstract: Saproxylic beetle is a public subject. The surface may be saproxylic beetles, but the interior is a problem of how slow forest degradation becomes comparable, memorable, and usable outside specialist settings. What this article has done is to take the ecological signals carried by beetles—forest continuity, deadwood habitat quality, drought pressure, microclimate buffering, coupled risk, pathogen windows, and fragmentation—and work them up as a web-based environmental media prototype, using Crete as a case. Buried in the five-scene interface grammar and the evidence-to-interface translation matrix is the plot of a slower crisis: deadwood continuity, refuge loss, threshold effects, and connectivity breakdown made visible without being turned into spectacle. The versioned publication model is similar, except that here there is an added insistence on archiving and uncertainty, so that causal inference remains possible without pretending to final certainty. In place of mascot logic or easy catastrophe, there is the more difficult public question of how indicator species can function as evidence interfaces. As its contribution to environmental media, the project raises the question—too rarely kept open in public forest communication—of how proxy variables, interaction sequences, and archivable formats can be lined up into an interpretable, comparable, and durable mode of reasoning for non-specialist publics. That, in turn, is its way of translating slow forest degradation into environmental media without surrendering auditability over time.

articles.title: Coastplay: a path-based public climate learning environment communication interface for coastal protected areas
articles.authors: JIAXI WANG
articles.abstract: Abstract: Coastplay is a route-based environmental communication prototype designed for public climate learning in collaboratively managed coastal protected areas. Rather than treating climate change as background information, the interface organizes local pressures, landform change, and multispecies relations into three optional learning paths: ocean, cliff, and sky. Each path uses low-threshold interactions such as dragging, clicking, and sliders to turn warming, storms, drought, and habitat change into comparable, discussable sequences. Its value lies in showing how co-management goals can be turned into a public-facing learning structure people can actually use. What emerges is less a digital tour guide than a way to build situated ecological literacy, conditional reasoning, and repeatable discussion.

articles.title: When Science Loses Its Immune System: Epistemic Immunodepression in the Age of AI
articles.authors: Tuyen Tran
articles.abstract: Clinical decisions depend on an evidence chain — from primary studies through systematic reviews to clinical guidelines — whose reliability rests on its capacity to detect and correct its own errors. I argue that artificial intelligence integration into research production is simultaneously degrading the four structural conditions that sustain this capacity: independent evaluation, methodological plurality, traceability, and epistemic friction — a syndrome I term epistemic immunodepression. Unlike visible AI errors such as hallucinated citations, this threat is structural: AI generates formally compliant outputs that bypass evaluation through methodological mimicry — the simulation of rigour without epistemic substance — and produces silent guideline drift, in which clinical recommendations shift without any detectable methodological violation. Medical-specific signals are accumulating: 28.6% to 91.4% of references generated by language models in systematic-review assistance are fabricated; only 6% of published AI models in paediatric surgery are both interpretable and externally validated; and an investigation of 2,271 evidence syntheses (2017–2024) documents the spread of automation across search, screening, and extraction. Current governance, anchored on voluntary disclosure, cannot detect these failure modes. The central move required is paradigmatic: from disclosure to verifiable provenance. Four structural interventions operationalise this shift — a research provenance record, a graded AI methodological logbook, recalibration of the evidence pyramid, and per-review AI accountability. A validation protocol has been pre-registered. If the diagnosis is confirmed, structural intervention is urgent — because a journal can retract a paper, but a surgeon cannot reverse a decision already executed on a child.

articles.title: Protocol for a systematic review on specialty-specific meta-research studies evaluating artificial intelligence author guidelines.
articles.authors: Shaan Mohan; Lik Hoong Chua; Ahmad Khan
articles.abstract: Recent meta-research studies have evaluated specialty-specific (i.e. pertaining to an individual medical specialty) author guidelines on the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in medical journals. To date, however, no systematic review has synthesised these findings; a review of this literature may help identify differences between specialties in the content and scope of AI-related guidance issued to authors. This systematic review aims to address this gap in the literature. This protocol has been described using the PRISMA-P statement. The primary outcomes will be measuring comparative prevalence of AI guidance domains across the journals linked to different medical specialties, including guidance on AI image processing, statistical analysis, and permitted or prohibited uses of AI in research (where such data is available). Secondary outcomes will describe the methodological design of the included studies.

articles.title: Reciprocity: A transparent universal credit system to incentivize peer review
articles.authors: Angela T Moles; Russell Bonduriansky; Stephen Bonser; Daniel Stein Falster; Shinichi Nakagawa; Nigel Andrew; Malgorzata Lagisz
articles.abstract: It’s increasingly hard to find peer reviewers. To address the problem, we propose a universal peer-review system based on the exchange of credits.

articles.title: RESTORING TRUST: A Comprehensive Audit of Research Integrity at the British Columbia Centre on Substance Use (2008–2025)
articles.authors: Ryan M. Moyer
articles.abstract:
Background:
The reproducibility crisis has emerged as a defining challenge in contemporary science, with mounting evidence of questionable research practices across disciplines. Substance use research, which directly informs life-and-death policy decisions for marginalized populations, remains particularly vulnerable to analytical flexibility that can compromise evidence quality. This study distinguishes researcher degrees of freedom (RDF) from analytical flexibility and operationalizes the latter through observable proxies to assess the methodological rigour of quantitative, regression-based studies conducted by the British Columbia Centre on Substance Use (BCCSU) between 2008 and 2025.
Methods:
We systematically reviewed 85 peer-reviewed publications affiliated with BCCSU researchers employing cross-sectional data from major cohorts (VIDUS, ACCESS, ARYS). Studies were assessed for pre-registration status, variable selection methods, transformation justifications, sampling timeframe rationale, missing data handling, and data/code transparency. Structured metadata extraction enabled quantification of researcher degrees of freedom across the institutional portfolio.
Results:
Zero studies (0%) were pre-registered on public platforms. Fifty-one publications (58%) relied on automated stepwise selection methods (AIC/QIC minimization) rather than theory-driven covariate specification (3.4%). Critical methodological decisions lacked transparency: 36% of studies employed unjustified variable transformations of drug use measures; 34% did not report missing data handling approaches; 66% provided no justification for sampling start dates; 69% provided no justification for sampling end dates. These researcher degrees of freedom produced pervasive analytic heterogeneity that rendered the evidence base largely unsynthesizable.

Conclusions:
The findings reveal systemic patterns consistent with institutional incentive structures prioritizing publication quantity over methodological rigour. Widespread reliance on data-driven model selection creates a high-risk environment for p-hacking and other questionable research practices. Comprehensive reform via pre-registration mandates, standardized reporting templates, and annual integrity metrics is required to restore public trust and produce policy-actionable science capable of serving vulnerable populations.

articles.title: Life Narratives and the Ten Aspects of the Big Five Across Open-ended and Targeted Prompts
articles.authors: Edward Chou; Noemi Isabel Hedrick; Dulce Wilkinson Westberg; Aisha Linnea Udochi; Matthew Rogers; Nicholas S. Holtzman; Moin Syed; Colin G. DeYoung
articles.abstract: Personality psychology seeks to understand not only individuals’ dispositional traits, but also components of personality like self-defining life narratives. Past studies correlating traits to narrative themes have largely focused on the Big Five. In the current study, two U.S. undergraduate samples (Study 1, N = 219; Study 2, N = 107) completed the Big Five Aspect Scales, which measure the Big Five domains and ten lower-order aspects (two per domain; e.g., Conscientiousness: Industriousness and Orderliness). Participants’ 2-page life stories (Study 1) and life high point, low point, and turning point narratives (Study 2) were coded for motivational, affective, structural, and autobiographical-reasoning themes. A mini meta-analysis across the two studies revealed several aspect-specific associations with motivational themes. For example, when controlling for shared variance between the aspects, Industriousness was positively associated with agency whereas Orderliness was negatively associated with agency. Trait associations with other narrative themes varied based on narrative methodology. In particular, the open-ended prompts and expanded coding schemes implemented in Study 1 may have allowed participants to better express their dispositional traits through narrative.

articles.title: Linearity-preserving scores for general error-prone measurements
articles.authors: Mathias Berggren; Jessica Kay Flake
articles.abstract: We present a model of how error-prone measurements with a certain expectation structure preserve the linear relationship between the latent error-free variables (Theorem 1). This also allows a simple relationship between latent and observed explained variances and correlations through the variance that remains in the measurements (Corollary 2), similar to the Spearman (1904) correction for classical errors. The model can be used to communicate the consequences of a broad set of measurement issues (Proposition 2), such as thresholding, misclassification, and missing data, through a single overarching analytical model. We also provide a conjecture (Conjecture 1) that justifies using this model to estimate latent OLS parameters under various error structures by using a 2x2-step Expectation-minimization (Em) algorithm. This works as an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm and consists of calculating expected values for observations with errors given a set of OLS parameters, and then minimizing the least-squares to update these parameters. We illustrate the estimation algorithm through two examples. In Example 1.1-4, we use it to estimate parameters in logistic regression, at baseline and with errors. In Example 2, we show how population information can be incorporated to account for errors.

articles.title: The WEIRD instrument problem and systematic bias in psychological research
articles.authors: Matthew Zefferman
articles.abstract: As researchers broaden samples to populations that are not Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD), they often use instruments designed for WEIRD populations. In this paper, I argue that this practice creates systematic biases that, for example, can explain consistent findings of less complex personality structures found outside WEIRD populations.  First, I model how administering instruments designed for one, typically WEIRD,  reference population to novel populations systematically underestimates the relative complexity of novel populations. This occurs because instruments optimized to measure the WEIRD population fail to capture the full structure of other populations. An additional outcome of the model was that a population's cultural distance from the reference population was strongly negatively associated with the measured personality structure complexity, even if the actual complexity for all populations was the same. I conducted an empirical analysis and found a negative association between cultural distance and personality structure complexity in pre-existing data. Consistent with the model’s prediction, cultural distance from the United States (the reference country for the personality instrument) had the strongest negative relationship with personality structure complexity of all 67 countries in the data. This was strong support for the hypothesis that lower apparent complexity outside WEIRD populations results from the use of WEIRD instruments, rather than from real differences in complexity. Modeling suggests that bias should occur whenever an instrument designed for one group, such as one gender or one cohort, is administered more widely. I propose possible reforms to avoid this type of bias.

articles.title: The Truly Horrific Face of Masculinity: Unravelling the Phenomenon of a Global Online Rape Academy
articles.authors: Andreas Walther; Katharina Haslacher
articles.abstract: This review elucidates the phenomenon of a global online rape academy through the lens of the psychology of men and masculinities. Triggered by recent investigative reports documenting online communities in which men exchanged strategies for sedating, sexually assaulting, recording, and distributing abuse against unconscious women, the article aims to move beyond explanations focused solely on individual pathology and instead explore broader psychosocial, cultural, and technological mechanisms that may contribute to such violence. From the outset of the Confluence Model of Sexual Aggression and drawing on the Gender Role Strain Paradigm (GRSP) and Precarious Manhood Theory (PMT), the article discusses how rigid masculinity norms emphasizing dominance, emotional restriction, entitlement, sexual conquest, and control over women may contribute to the normalization and reinforcement of misogynistic violence within digitally connected peer environments. Additional concepts, including masculine sexual entitlement, narcissistic entitlement, externalizing forms of male distress, as well as technology acceptance frameworks, are integrated to illustrate how online platforms can facilitate anonymity, group validation, tactical coordination, and the amplification of violent masculine performances. The article further highlights important explanatory aspects differing from the Confluence Model of Sexual Aggression and argues that these online networks represent not merely isolated deviance, but socially reinforced ecosystems in which violence becomes normalized, rewarded, and collectively organized. At the same time, the paper emphasizes that masculinity itself is not inherently violent and that the overwhelming majority of men do not engage in sexual violence. Understanding the psychological and sociocultural mechanisms contributing to such extreme forms of abuse may nevertheless help improve prevention, victim protection, digital platform regulation, and international criminal prosecution, while fostering healthier and less harmful models of masculinity.

articles.title: Using Experience Sampling Methods to Study Identity Dynamics: A 5-Step Guide
articles.authors: Zhenchao Hu; Anthony Ong; Nilam Ram
articles.abstract: A dynamic perspective on identity is essential for explaining how identities emerge, stabilize, and change across everyday situations. Mounting theory and evidence show that identity varies meaningfully from moment to moment. Experience sampling methods (ESM), a data collection method where participants repeatedly provide researchers with information about their current or recent thoughts, behaviors, feelings, and contexts as they happen in real-time, is facilitating identification and documentation of intraindividual identity dynamics and the interindividual differences in those dynamics. This paper provides a five-step practical guide to ESM – full flow from theoretical specification to study design, data collection, data analysis, and results presentation and interpretation – that may be helpful for identity scholars seeking to integrate ESM in their work. In presenting each step we outline the core goals and decisions involved and provide an illustrative example, describing how specific design decisions were made in a small empirical study of how ethnic-racial and gender identity change across social situations, and how the resulting specifics and empirical results might be reported in a journal manuscript. Together with accompanying R code implementations, this guide expands identity researchers’ toolbox and the exploration and study of intraindividual identity dynamics.

articles.title: A bad apple may not spoil the barrel: Mechanisms of trust formation under  misinformation exposure
articles.authors: Gabriel Braun; Ruth Mayo; NA
articles.abstract:
Under widespread misinformation, deciding which news sources to trust is consequential. 
Here, we examine the mechanisms people use to form trust in news sources. Across 8 
experiments, participants continuously evaluated a sequence of news posts that included one 
piece of misinformation. Then, they indicated trust in the source. For neutral content 
(Experiments 1-5), trust tracked the average reliability of the reports, whether the source was 
a news channel (Experiments 1-3) or an individual (Experiments 4-5). Despite clear detection 
of misinformation, there was no evidence for a “one strike and you’re out” mechanism. In 
contrast, for politically charged content (Experiments 6-7), Republicans continued to rely on 
averaging, whereas Democrats shifted to a mechanism that heavily penalized misinformation, 
differences that we discuss further. These findings suggest that, for neutral content, trust 
judgments follow equal-weighting strategy, but political priors can shape not only belief in 
specific reports, but the process of trusting news.

articles.title: Investigating the Influence of Spatial Acoustic Attributes and Visual Cues on Psychoacoustic Annoyance
articles.authors: Samuel Dickinson; Hyunkook Lee
articles.abstract: The influence of spatial acoustic attributes on psychoacoustic annoyance remains insufficiently understood. This study investigated how source direction, acoustic condition and visual cues affect perceived annoyance using a virtual reality-based experimental method. Four common domestic sound sources (emergency alarm, coffee grinder, traffic noise and mechanical keyboard) were rendered in three acoustic conditions: anechoic, small office and large café. Higher-order ambisonic room impulse responses were captured using a spherical microphone array and reproduced binaurally with head tracking in a virtual reality (VR) environment. Thirty-three participants freely rotated their heads to identify the most and least annoying source directions for each stimulus and rated annoyance on an 11-point scale. Results confirmed that sound sources were generally perceived as most annoying when positioned laterally, at around ±90° relative to the listener, and least annoying when positioned either directly in front of or behind the listener. Visual cues influenced the directional responses by providing visual anchors and reducing uncertainty in source localisation, although they did not significantly affect the overall annoyance level at the most and least annoying positions. Acoustic condition significantly affected annoyance for some sources, with the office environment producing greater annoyance than the café in several cases, potentially due to both acoustic characteristics and contextual associations. The findings demonstrate that annoyance perception is shaped not only by the sound source itself, but also by source direction, room acoustics, visual context and psychological interpretation of the environment. The proposed VR-based method provides an ecologically valid approach for studying spatial and multisensory factors in psychoacoustic annoyance and has potential applications in soundscape evaluation and environmental design.

articles.title: A meta-analytic database of 789 choice architecture interventions from 280 experiments coded on 298 variables
articles.authors: Linnea Gandhi; Anoushka Kiyawat; Elizabeth Tipton; Duncan J Watts
articles.abstract: The impact of choice architecture interventions—psychologically-informed changes to communications, layouts, and processes—is context dependent. How well a given intervention works to nudge a given behavior depends on who is being nudged, where, when, by whom, and to do what. In order to help researchers explore the context-dependency of interventions, we built a database of 789 interventions from 280 randomized controlled experiments published between 2018 and 2023 by academic and practitioner sources. We developed a custom coding infrastructure to encode 298 variables that describe the domain, geography, population, behavior, intervention techniques, and standardized effect sizes, as well as variables describing elements of the experimental design. Variables come from multiple behavioral science taxonomies and cover binary, continuous, and qualitative values. Coding followed a series of quality checks across inclusion, extraction, consensus, audit, and author review. The analysis-ready database, a digital repository of visual intervention stimuli, coding templates and processes, relevant code, and an interactive explorer are deposited as open resources. [Publicly available after peer review.]

articles.title: Measuring Cybersickness in Virtual Reality: Refinement and Validation of the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire
articles.authors: Jonathan Kelly; Taylor Doty; Stephen B. Gilbert; Michael Dorneich
articles.abstract:
Objective
The Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) is widely used to assess cybersickness, but its structure and scoring may not fully reflect symptom patterns experienced in modern virtual reality (VR). This study evaluated whether a simpler restructured version could provide a more streamlined and interpretable measure while preserving its association with behavioral tolerance.

Background
Cybersickness includes symptoms such as nausea, disorientation, and eyestrain. Existing SSQ scoring uses overlapping subscales, potentially redundant items, and weighted scores that may reduce interpretability.

Method
Study 1 (N = 361) used exploratory factor analysis (EFA) on 16 SSQ items administered following VR exposure. Study 2 (N = 481) used confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) in an independent sample to compare competing models. Criterion-related validity was evaluated using behavioral tolerance measures (time in VR and early withdrawal).

Results
EFA supported a three-factor structure (strain, dizziness/disorientation, nausea). Removing weak and redundant items produced an 11-item model (VR-SSQ). CFA indicated acceptable fit, comparable to the original SSQ and alternative scoring methods. The VR-SSQ uses non-overlapping subscales scored as simple item means. All measures were strongly associated with reduced behavioral tolerance to VR (shorter exposure duration and increased likelihood of withdrawal).

Conclusion
Cybersickness reflects a multidimensional construct with a strong general component. The VR-SSQ is a reduced item set that captures this structure while retaining comparable associations with behavioral tolerance, supporting a simpler and more interpretable measurement tool.

Application
The streamlined VR-SSQ facilitates more interpretable assessment of cybersickness symptoms while reducing time to administer and scoring complexity in research and applied VR settings.

articles.title: Rhythm Abstraction in Bumble Bees Remains Inconclusive
articles.authors: Zhi-Yuan Ning; Carel ten Cate; Aniruddh D. Patel; Henkjan Honing
articles.abstract: Zeng et al. (2026) report rhythm abstraction in bumble bees. While we applaud this effort to expand the examination of rhythm perception into the insect clade, we argue that the evidence for rhythmic generalization remains inconclusive. The stimuli used to test tempo-invariance were not proportional transformations, as inter-flash gaps remained fixed across conditions. Discrimination in Experiment 3 could be explained by simpler local perceptual cues or absolute duration matching rather than global rhythmic patterns. The claim that “bumble bees also form robust abstract rhythm representations” is thus not supported by the study.

articles.title: Personalized Prediction of Momentary Affect with Passive Smartphone Sensing Data in a Clinically Heterogeneous Sample: An evaluation of machine learning and mixed effects approaches
articles.authors: Grant Alexander King; Colin Vize; Adriene M. Beltz; Aidan G.C. Wright
articles.abstract: Passive smartphone sensing offers a scalable, low-burden approach to continuous mental health monitoring, yet most studies which use passive sensing features to predict momentary psychological states rely on small, clinically homogeneous samples, limiting generalizability and clinical utility. This study compares methods for personalized prediction of momentary affect, stress, and fatigue using passive sensing in a large, clinically diverse sample of 308 adults (66% with a psychiatric diagnosis). Participants completed 15 days of ecological momentary assessment (23,560 total observations) with passive sensing of GPS, accelerometer, phone call, screen-on-time, and sleep data. We predicted five continuous outcomes: positive affect, negative affect, stress, energy, and a depression composite. We explored methods for balancing group-level and personalized prediction, including mixed effects regression tree (MERT) boosting, a method that blends mixed effects and machine learning approaches. We compared this to group-level pooled models, fully personalized models, and linear mixed models. A key finding was that the use of cluster-mean centering (CMC) as a preprocessing step dramatically improved pooled machine learning model performance, enabling a simple XGBoost model to match or exceed fully personalized and MERT boosting approaches. Best models compared favorably to past work predicting continuous momentary affect, achieving overall R-squared of 0.38-0.49 and average idiographic R-squared of 0.16-0.26 calculated on held out test data, with energy and the depression composite emerging as the most predictable outcomes. These results carry direct clinical implications: scalable group-level models can rival personalized models when data are appropriately preprocessed, lowering barriers to real-world deployment of passive sensing–based monitoring. The high predictability of energy and the depressive composite highlights viable targets for just-in-time adaptive interventions. This work advances digital phenotyping by demonstrating clinically relevant affect prediction at scale and identifying CMC as a critical tool for scalable personalization of mental health monitoring.

articles.title: Adaptive Intuitions Shape Susceptibility to Misinformation
articles.authors: Reed Orchinik; Cameron Martel; David Gertler Rand; Rahul Bhui
articles.abstract: Belief in misinformation has been attributed to digital media environments that promote intuitive thinking, which is thought to foster uncritical acceptance of content. We propose that this intuitive "truth-bias" may be an ecological adaptation to environments where information is typically accurate. Across a large-scale pre-registered survey experiment and an incentivized replication, we test whether intuitions indeed adapt to the base rate of true versus false content. Participants viewed news feeds composed primarily of either true or false headlines. We find that individuals make more - and faster - errors when encountering the less frequent headline type, and fewer errors on the more common type. Computational modeling of the cognitive process reveals these effects are driven by intuitive responses that function like Bayesian priors about content accuracy, which exhibit some persistence. Our findings suggest that susceptibility to misinformation may not merely reflect a cognitive failure, but rather a byproduct of learning from statistical regularities in digital environments.

articles.title: Mapping narrative reports of drug induced experiences: A data-driven dictionary-based approach.
articles.authors: Joseph Peter Rennie; Celia Morgan
articles.abstract: Firsthand accounts of drug users’ experiences offer a rich resource to enhance our understanding of the effects of different substances. Advances in natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML) techniques offer promising ways to interrogate these subjective reports at scale. The present study applies a broad-scope, data-driven, dictionary-based approach to a large corpus spanning 56 substances across multiple pharmacological classes. Using a pipeline combining random forest classification and k-means clustering, we identified 22-word categories most informative for differentiating substances and examined how substances grouped according to shared linguistic profiles. Three broad clusters emerged: a Hedonic-social cluster characterised by social, motivational, and affective language; a Pharmaceutical-instrumental cluster marked by biological, somatic, affective, and functional language; and a Psychedelic-like cluster distinguished by perceptual, spatial, and insight-related language. These groupings show areas of convergence with prior classifications alongside informative divergences, suggesting that word-category frequencies capture meaningful but coarse-grained regularities. Notably, clusters did not map neatly onto pharmacological classes alone but instead appear to reflect a combination of pharmacological features, phenomenological features, contextual and functional use patterns, and motivational orientations. While abstraction from broader semantic context introduces ambiguity and warrants cautious interpretation, the present findings demonstrate that data-driven word-category features can reveal structured patterns across a diverse range of substances. Such approaches show potential for continued refinement and offer a broad-stroke entry point into large-scale semantic mapping that can serve as a scaffold for progressively finer-grained, theory-informed, and multi-method investigations of drug induced experiences.

articles.title: Mesmer’s error: Beliefs reverse the rubber hand illusion (a Registered Report)
articles.authors: Peter Lush; Zoltan Dienes
articles.abstract: In the rubber hand illusion (RHI), participants report ownership of a fake hand which is stroked in synchrony with their own concealed hand, with lower ownership ratings for asynchronous stroking. This effect has been historically attributed to multisensory integration. However, such a pattern of results is consistent with experimental demand characteristics, and an alternative theory proposes that the RHI is an effect of phenomenological control (PC; the trait ability to change experience to satisfy goals). To date, support for a PC theory of the RHI has been limited to correlational evidence. Here we conduct a pre-registered causal test of the relative contribution of these two proposed mechanisms in RHI effects. 25 high PC participants (top 15%) were tested in an RHI procedure and the classic effects of greater response for synchrony than asynchrony were replicated. The procedure was then repeated following imaginative suggestions for a reversal of the classic effect (i.e. greater synchrony than asynchrony effects). Ratings of ownership and proprioceptive drift were modulated by suggestion. Effects for ownership and drift were reversed. Exploratory analysis showed the same pattern of results for referred touch ratings. Participant beliefs arising from demand characteristics dominate over multisensory mechanisms in RHI ownership of a fake hand and proprioceptive drift. Multisensory synchrony is neither necessary nor sufficient for the rubber hand illusion. Claims of an illusion beyond that attributable to demand characteristics and phenomenological control require experimental evidence from controlled experiments

articles.title: Tiny Alchemists: The Developmental Emergence of Empowerment in Exploration
articles.authors: Franziska Brändle; Azzurra Ruggeri; Silja Keßler; Eric Schulz
articles.abstract: How do children's exploration strategies develop? Prior work has shown that children explore more broadly than adults, often driven by uncertainty. Yet, little is known about how more sophisticated strategies, such as empowerment - i.e. seeking actions that expand future possibilities - unfold across development. We investigated this in two experiments using a complex combinatorial game ("Tiny Alchemy''). In Experiment 1, we found that empowerment use increases with age, while uncertainty decreased. Young children (5 - 8 years) primarily relied on uncertainty, while older children (9 - 13 years) and adults used both uncertainty empowerment. In Experiment 2, we asked whether younger children's limited use of empowerment reflected different semantic intuitions. Results showed that older children used empowerment but could not explicitly judge it, while adults both used and accurately assessed empowerment. These findings suggest that empowerment emerges gradually: first as an implicit strategy in late childhood, and only later becoming explicit and verbalizable.

articles.title: Children Encounter Rich Language Input Through Video Media
articles.authors: Anna Gowenlock; Jennifer M Rodd; Courtenay Norbury; Beth Malory
articles.abstract: Recent corpus studies suggest that vocabulary in children’s books is more complex than vocabulary in day-to-day conversations (Montag et al., 2015; Dawson et al., 2021). Less is known about the language of children’s video media, a highly prevalent source of language input for young children. In this study we compare the Children’s Corpus of Video Media (CCVM: a specialised corpus of videos popular among UK pre-schoolers; ~233,000 words) to a large database of child-directed speech (CDS; ~2,589,000 words). Compared to CDS, the vocabulary in the CCVM is more diverse, sophisticated, and richer in meaning. Words that are distinctive of video language have a higher age of acquisition than CDS words, but do not differ in concreteness. These findings support the theory that rich vocabulary may arise from narrative formats, and so, like book language, video media may provide rich lexical input for young children.

articles.title: Making Food VAT Reform Tangible: Concrete Price Information Increases Public Acceptance
articles.authors: Hellen Temme; Cornelia Betsch; Philipp Sprengholz
articles.abstract: Increasing the value-added tax (VAT) on animal- and reducing it on plant-based foods can reduce greenhouse gas emissions and diet-related disease burden by shifting consumption toward more sustainable dietary patterns. Yet, public acceptance remains low. Two pre-registered online experiments in Germany examined whether acceptance is linked to cost overestimation and whether communicating accurate price information increases public support. In Study 1 (N = 161), participants submitted a personal grocery receipt and were randomly assigned to one of three VAT scenarios: an increase on animal-based products (7% to 19%), a reduction on plant-based products (7% to 0%), or both. Policy acceptance was measured before and after participants estimated the price change of their receipt and received feedback on the actual change. Although participants systematically misestimated costs, initial acceptance (β = .92, p < .001) rather than misestimation (β = .03, p = .329) was the dominant predictor of subsequent acceptance. When given the opportunity to eliminate products from their receipt after receiving feedback on the actual change, participants predominantly removed meat and dairy. Study 2 (N = 299) focused on the VAT combination policy using a standardized €30 receipt. Participants either estimated costs before receiving feedback, received accurate price information only, or received only a policy description. Acceptance in the estimation (d = .54) and information (d = .33) conditions significantly increased compared to the control condition, while misestimation showed no significant relationship with acceptance. These findings suggest that expressing price changes in Euros rather than VAT percentages represents a simple, scalable strategy for increasing public support for sustainable food taxation.

articles.title: Latent Profiles of Personality and Imaginative Thinking: Student Differences in Enrollment Across College Disciplines
articles.authors: Kristen Lamb; Lindsay Ellis Lee
articles.abstract: In a society where creativity is increasingly emphasized, imagination remains underexamined despite its foundational role in creative processes. This study used a person-centered approach to examine latent personality and imaginative capability profiles, sex differences, and how these profiles relate to students’ enrollment across college disciplines. Latent Profile Analysis identified three typologies among university students: Profile 1, Reactive Realists (10.5%), characterized by lower imagination and emotional stability; Profile 2, Reserved Idealists (53.3%), characterized by lower extraversion and moderately elevated imagination; and Profile 3, Balanced Visionaries (36.2%), characterized by high imagination and greater emotional stability. Profiles were primarily differentiated by overall levels rather than distinct patterns across traits. Although chi-square analyses indicated statistically significant differences in profile membership by sex, these effects were small. Multinomial regression analyses showed that profile membership, sex, and race/ethnicity were associated with discipline enrollment. Balanced Visionaries had lower odds of enrollment in the College of Science relative to Liberal Arts and Social Sciences. Females were less likely to enroll in Business, Engineering, and Music, but more likely to enroll in Education and Visual Arts and Design. These findings highlight the value of person-centered approaches while also showing that sex and race more consistently predict college discipline enrollment.

articles.title: Science and Different Ways of Knowing
articles.authors: Patricia Nayna Schwerdtle; Justin McBrayer; Patrick Casey; Michael J. Strambler
articles.abstract: This paper examines the growing prominence of “different ways of knowing” across disciplines including psychology, global health, and environmental science, and clarifies their relationship to scientific inquiry. While recognising the legitimate motivations underlying this trend - particularly concerns about exclusion, epistemic injustice, and the need for more inclusive and context-sensitive research - the paper argues that these aims do not require the adoption of alternative epistemic systems alongside science. Drawing on philosophy of science, the authors distinguish between diverse sources of insight and the methodological standards that govern the production of empirical knowledge. They propose a set of thresholds for identifying when the boundary between science and non-science is crossed, including claims of epistemic parity, the insulation of claims from criticism, and institutional enforcement of such positions. The paper argues that maintaining shared standards of evidence - such as testability, openness to refutation, and collective error correction - is essential for science to function as a public, cross-cultural enterprise, while still allowing meaningful integration of diverse perspectives as inputs to, rather than substitutes for, scientific inquiry.

articles.title: Family language policies in Nordfriesland and Fryslân:  A study of parental language attitudes and the intergenerational transmission of Frisian
articles.authors: Ruth Kircher; Suzanne Dekker; Jelske Dijkstra; Pavlina Heinzova; Mirjam Vellinga
articles.abstract: The study presented here investigates family language policies in the district of Nordfriesland in Germany and the province of Fryslân in the Netherlands. The focus is on parents’ language beliefs (specifically, their attitudes towards Frisian) and how these influence their language practices (specifically, the intergenerational transmission of Frisian). The analyses examine data from 659 Frisian-speaking parents of young children across both contexts, gathered with the Adapted Parental Attitudes Towards Languages Questionnaire. The results reveal that these parents hold more positive solidarity-related attitudes towards Frisian than towards the local majority language (German in Germany and Dutch in the Netherlands), but more positive status-related attitudes towards the local majority language than towards Frisian. Parents in Nordfriesland hold even less positive status-related attitudes towards Frisian than parents in Fryslân, reflecting the comparative difference in utilitarian value Frisian holds in these contexts. Notably, both status-related and solidarity-related attitudes are found to predict the intergenerational transmission of Frisian. The findings from this study have both theoretical and practical implications: they advance our understanding of family language policies in (hitherto under-researched) autochthonous heritage language contexts, and they facilitate the development of support measures for parents raising their children multilingually with Frisian as one of their languages.

articles.title: Mind the Research Culture Gap: Staff Perspectives on Ethical, Inclusive, and Sustainable Research Practice at a UK Research Intensive University
articles.authors: Samuel Finnerty; Yingnian Tao; Heather Shaw; Richard Philpot; Nuri Kwon; Seth Robinson; Mark Levine
articles.abstract: Research culture reform increasingly requires researchers to attend to ethical, inclusive, and sustainable practice, yet little is known about how staff perceive the gap between what is valued and what is enacted. This mixed-methods study surveyed 244 staff at a UK research-intensive university, comparing ratings of how important each practice should be (ideal) with perceptions of how far it is currently realised (actual) across ethics, inclusion (operationalised as community-based research), and sustainability. Ratings were collected at three actor levels, the participant, their peers, and their institution. This allowed an examination of where university staff identify the largest research culture change barriers. Paired-samples t-tests revealed significant ideal-actual gaps across all domains, with sustainability showing the largest differences and ethics the smallest. A consistent self-other narrative emerged: participants rated their own practice most favourably, with the largest gaps between their own practice and that of their peers. For ethics, the individual-level gap was negligible, consistent with the procedural formalisation of ethical review. For sustainability, gaps were substantial at every level. Open-text responses contextualised these patterns, revealing that ethical practice was framed as individually controllable, while inclusion was seen as context-dependent and collectively constrained, and sustainability practice as routinely blocked by structural barriers. These findings suggest that procedural formalisation can narrow the ideal-actual gap in research culture change but may foster surface compliance rather than reflexive engagement. We conclude that the path to research culture change, particularly in respect of sustainability and inclusion, requires structural reform as well as individual behaviour change.

articles.title: Evaluating Local Structural-After-Measurement (LSAM) and Traditional Approaches for the Estimation of Complex Nonlinear Effects Among Latent Variables
articles.authors: Felipe Fontana Vieira; KJell Slupphaug; Yves Rosseel
articles.abstract:
Various methods exist to include quadratic or interaction terms involving latent
variables in structural equation models (SEM). Most use system-wide estimation,
where all parameters are estimated simultaneously, and have been investigated in
models with few nonlinear effects. Recently, structural-after-measurement (SAM)
approaches have been proposed, where estimation proceeds in two stages:
measurement model first, then structural model. Rosseel et al. (2025) extended local
SAM (LSAM) to handle second-order nonlinear effects among latent variables. In this
article, we present a formula for two-step standard errors (SEs) not previously available
for LSAM. We also conducted two simulation studies varying latent exogenous
predictor distributions (normal, right-skewed, uniform), reliabilities (0.4, 0.6, 0.8),
sample sizes (400, 1000), and measurement error and structural disturbance
distributions (normal, right-skewed). The first study examined a model with three
nonlinear effects. LSAM produced largely unbiased estimates with stable coverage,
SEs, and Type I error rates, though power fluctuated and performance was adversely
affected under low reliability, particularly with uniform latent exogenous distributions.
Larger sample sizes mitigated these issues. Traditional methods (LMS, QML, UPI)
showed results consistent with prior literature. The second study, excluding LMS,
tested a more complex model with eight nonlinear effects. LSAM maintained adequate
performance despite greater complexity, though similar limitations emerged and power
decreased under certain conditions. Traditional methods yielded more variable results,
with generally poorer performance relative to Simulation 1 and LSAM. Right-skewed
measurement errors affected the abovementioned results more than right-skewed
structural disturbances, particularly under low and medium reliability.

articles.title: A Theory-Driven Prefrontal Cortex ROI Library for Applied fNIRS: Confirmatory Functional Connectivity Evidence from NIRSIT Lite
articles.authors: Jerome I. Rotgans
articles.abstract: Standardized region-of-interest (ROI) definitions for the prefrontal cortex (PFC) are important for reliable functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) applications in real-world settings. Many existing fNIRS connectivity approaches rely on exploratory clustering or passive paradigms, which can make ROI definitions dependent on the sampled dataset rather than on prior theory. In this study, we present a theory-driven, confirmatory ROI library for the PFC using the NIRSIT Lite channel layout. The library is grounded in established neuroscience literature on executive control, visual perceptual decision-making, strategic planning, uncertainty processing, and cognitive flexibility. Across 70 high-cognitive-load datasets, within-ROI correlations (mean r = .46) were significantly higher than between-ROI correlations (mean r = .33), with a mean difference of .13 (p < .0001, Cohen’s d = 1.91). These findings provide confirmatory functional-connectivity evidence for the internal coherence of the proposed ROI structure and offer a transparent alternative to purely exploratory channel clustering.

articles.title: ‘Sustainable Choices’ - An Intervention to Promote Climate Change Mitigation Behaviours: A Randomized Controlled Trial
articles.authors: Fabian Lenhard; Lorena Fernández de la Cruz; Francesco Fuso Nerini; Katarina Axelsson; David Mataix-Cols
articles.abstract:
Background: Individual climate change mitigation behaviours (CCMBs) can meaningfully reduce greenhouse gas emissions, yet many people report uncertainty about what actions to take and difficulty changing established habits. Scalable, digital behavioural interventions remain largely untested. This randomized controlled trial evaluated the effectiveness and acceptability of Sustainable Choices, a five-week, internet-delivered intervention designed to promote CCMBs and psychological well-being.

Methods: Adults in Sweden (N = 694) were randomized 1:1 to Sustainable Choices or a waitlist control. The primary outcome was CCMBs at post-intervention, assessed with the Climate Mitigation Behaviour Scale (CLIMBS) and analysed using item-response-theory–derived latent scores in mixed-effects models under an intention-to-treat framework. Secondary outcomes included well-being (SWEMWBS), climate-related worry (GAD-2), low mood (PHQ-2), and meaning and purpose (PROMIS-6a). Follow-ups were conducted at 1, 3, and 6 months. Complier Average Causal Effect analyses examined dose–response relations, and acceptability was assessed through adherence and satisfaction.

Results: Compared with controls, the intervention produced significantly greater increases in CCMBs on the CLIMBS general factor (d = 0.34, p < .001), with improvements in food, consumption, and engagement domains but not mobility. Meaning and purpose increased relative to controls (d = 0.23, p = .004), whereas changes in well-being, worry, and low mood were not significant. Engagement showed robust dose-dependent effects: each completed module increased CCMBs by 0.09 points, and full adherence yielded a 0.45-point gain. Satisfaction was high, with 84% recommending the course.

Conclusions: Sustainable Choices achieved small-to-moderate improvements in climate mitigation behaviours and enhanced perceived meaning and purpose. Fully digital behavioural interventions may provide a scalable complement to broader climate strategies, meriting further evaluation in diverse populations and with objective behavioural measures.

articles.title: Cognitive functioning and Anxiety/Depression: Accounting for Genetic Confounding in the ALSPAC Cohort.
articles.authors: Susan Robinson; Jiayao Xu; Hannah Jones; Ian Penton-Voak; Laura D. Howe
articles.abstract:
Aims: Depression and anxiety are leading contributors to the global burden of disease, yet the mechanisms underlying these conditions, including the role of cognitive processes, remain uncertain. Cognitive deficits in domains such as emotion recognition, working memory, and response inhibition are commonly observed in individuals with depression and anxiety, but it is unclear whether these reflect causal risk factors, consequences of symptoms, or shared aetiological influences. We investigated whether associations between cognitive function and mental health outcomes persist after accounting for genetic confounding using data from the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC).

Methods: Participants completed computer-based assessments of emotion recognition, working memory, and response inhibition at age 24. Depression and anxiety diagnoses were derived at age 24 using the Clinical Interview Schedule - Revised. Logistic regression models examined associations between cognitive function and each mental health outcome, followed by models adjusting for sociodemographic confounders, cognitive confounders, and polygenic scores (PGS) for depression and anxiety. Genetic sensitivity analyses (Gsens) were conducted to estimate the extent to which associations were attributable to shared genetic liability.

Results: Higher working memory performance was consistently associated with lower odds of anxiety, and this association was robust to adjustment for observed confounders and PGS, with Gsens analyses indicating no evidence of genetic confounding. Evidence for an association between working memory and depression was weak and disappeared after accounting for genetic confounding. Emotion recognition showed heterogeneous patterns: better sadness recognition was associated with higher risk of depression, while weaker happiness recognition showed preliminary associations with depression but these were fully explained by shared genetic influences. For anxiety, genetic sensitivity analyses suggested negative (masking) genetic confounding for some emotion recognition domains, such that associations became stronger after accounting for genetic liability. Slower response inhibition showed weak associations with depression, which Gsens analyses suggested were entirely attributable to genetic confounding.

Conclusions: Overall, these findings indicate that the relationship between working memory and anxiety is unlikely to be explained by shared genetic liability, supporting its plausibility as an intervention target. In contrast, several associations involving emotion recognition and response inhibition with depression/ anxiety appear to reflect shared genetic origins.

articles.title: Midfrontal theta power relates to response speeding following frustrative nonreward
articles.authors: Nellia Bellaert; Federico Cassioli; Michael Crowley; Hilary P Blumberg; Mandy Rossignol; Christen M Deveney; Wan-Ling Tseng
articles.abstract: Objectives: Irritability is a transdiagnostic symptom proposed to be characterized by maladaptive responses to frustrative nonreward (FNR), including heightened negative affect (i.e., frustration). Although FNR typically elicits adaptive behavioral adjustments, the neural mechanisms that support these adjustments and their trial-by-trial dynamics remain unexplored. Midfrontal theta power (MFθ), a key marker of expectancy violation and cognitive control, is a strong candidate for indexing these mechanisms and their role in supporting adaptive responses. We investigated how fluctuations in MFθ during FNR relate to subsequent behavioral adjustments and whether these associations are moderated by individual differences in irritability and state frustration. Methods: Young adults (N = 91) completed a validated frustration paradigm and self-reported their irritability and state frustration. A multivariate approach was used to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio of single-trial MFθ. Linear mixed-effects models tested whether trial-by-trial MFθ and feedback type (win vs. FNR) predicted reaction time on the following trial. Results: Higher MFθ following FNR feedback, but not win feedback, was associated with faster reaction times on the next trial, suggesting that theta oscillations support adaptive behavioral adjustments to unexpected reward loss. Reduced MFθ to FNR feedback was linked to greater self-reported frustration, while trait irritability and state frustration did not moderate the MFθ-behavior association. Conclusion: MFθ increased following FNR feedback and predicted faster subsequent responses, highlighting its role in adaptive adjustment to frustrative outcomes. These findings enhance understanding of the neurocognitive mechanisms supporting coping with frustration and suggest this process may be less effective under heightened subjective frustration.

articles.title: The Promise and Peril of Social Media: How Context Shapes Online Risks and Digital Literacy Among Marginalised Adolescents in a Semi-Rural South African Community
articles.authors: Mienke Mari Steytler; Douglas A. Parry
articles.abstract: Debates about online risks often overlook the realities of young people navigating structural adversity and inequality. This qualitative study explores how adolescents in a low-income, semi-rural South African community navigate the double-edged nature of social media. Drawing on in-depth interviews, we show how offline violence, gendered power dynamics, and structural inequalities are entangled with online experiences, creating overlapping risks that amplify existing vulnerabilities. Simultaneously, social media is a source of entertainment, belonging, and glimpses of alternative futures, particularly for adolescents with personal smartphone access. Digital literacy in this context is informal and reactive, shaped by peers and trusted adults rather than formal education. These findings show how local contexts structure both the benefits and harms of social media as well as the forms of digital literacy that develop.

articles.title: A Complex Dynamic Systems Theory Approach to Second Language Development
articles.authors: Diane Larsen-Freeman; Ali H. Al-Hoorie; Phil V. Hiver
articles.abstract: In this chapter, the authors provide an overview of core theoretical concepts of Complex Dynamic Systems Theory (CDST) and the specific ways its principles apply to language development and language use. The chapter also presents supporting empirical evidence for specific developmental processes that language learners experience and surveys compatible methods used in second language development research. Finally, the chapter highlights commonalities and differences with other approaches in this volume and points to future directions that will help extend existing insights from CDST in understanding key processes of language development.

articles.title: Biased and Inattentive Responding Drive Apparent Metacognitive Biases in Mental Health
articles.authors: Noam Sarna; Reuven Dar; Matan Mazor
articles.abstract: Large-scale online studies with healthy adults have documented consistent associations between transdiagnostic psychiatric traits and metacognitive biases. Here, analysis of existing and new large-scale datasets reveals that such correlations are largely driven by surface-level dimensions of questionnaire-filling behaviour: systematic rating biases and inattentive responding. Specifically, a bias to report positive or negative values in self-report scales generalizes to confidence ratings, producing spurious correlations between the two. Additionally, systematic over-confidence among inattentive responders produces spurious positive correlations between confidence and the endorsement of rare symptoms. We show that previously identified transdiagnostic dimensions of “anxiety-depression” and “compulsivity and intrusive thought,” both shown to correlate with decision confidence, map neatly onto these two biases of questionnaire-filling behaviour. In a pre-registered experiment, we further show that decision confidence and self-reported obsessive-compulsive tendencies are correlated with independent measures of inattentive and biased responding. Taken together, we find an alarming degree of influence of inattentive and biased responding over both self-report psychiatric measures and confidence ratings. When not accounted for, these factors produce a mirage of apparent metacognitive alterations in mental health. We discuss concrete precautionary measures that are needed to control for these biases.

articles.title: Of Breathing and Lying:  The (Null) Effect of a Brief Mindfulness Meditation on Immoral Behavior at Work
articles.authors: Simon Schindler; Julia Schindler; Stefan Pfattheicher; Jan Lehrke; Marc-André Reinhard
articles.abstract: Mindfulness practices have been linked to enhanced emotional regulation, cognitive control and, consequently, prosocial behavior. However, these regulatory capacities may also facilitate coping with guilt and stress following immoral actions. The present research tested whether a brief mindfulness meditation facilitates more successful lying about a bullying incident at work. In this experiment, 250 participants were randomly assigned to one of three mental exercise conditions (mindfulness, mind-wandering, positive affirmation). In a workplace-bullying cover-up paradigm, participants delivered either a false or a true statement in response to an accusation of having bullied a colleague at work. Participants who bullied a colleague and then lied about it reported significantly higher feelings of guilt and stress compared to those who told the truth about not bullying. However, this effect was not significantly moderated by mental exercise. In a follow-up study, 171 independent observers viewed the recorded statements and judged their veracity. As expected, observers were more likely to judge true statements as true compared to false statements (i.e., a stronger truth bias when judging true statements). Again, however, this effect was not significantly moderated by mental exercise. These findings suggest limited effects of brief mindfulness meditations on internal regulation and on advantages in lying.

articles.title: The impact of privacy risk perception on initial trust in autonomous vehicle: Differential responses of professionals and non-professionals
articles.authors: Yifei Sun; Xiulan Li; Feng Du; Yue Qi
articles.abstract: Previous research has primarily focused on under-trust in autonomous driving systems. However, over-trust among ordinary consumers may likewise lead to system misuse and increased usage risks. Drawing on three empirical studies, the present research systematically examined the influence of professional background on initial trust, with a particular focus on comparing and calibrating the trust levels of ordinary consumers to be closer to those of industry professionals as domain experts. Study 1 found that non-professionals exhibited a tendency toward over-trust, and that perceived privacy risk interacted with professional background in predicting initial trust. Study 2 further manipulated privacy risk levels and revealed differential responses between professionals and non-professionals. Specifically, increased risk levels significantly heightened non-professionals’ perceived privacy risk and reduced their initial trust, whereas professionals’ initial trust was comparatively less affected by changes in risk levels. Study 3 further revealed an asymmetric response among non-professionals to privacy risk information. Under low-risk conditions, although perceived privacy risk increased significantly, initial trust showed no significant change. In contrast, under high-risk conditions, perceived privacy risk increased significantly while initial trust decreased significantly. Taken together, these findings reveal the interactive effects of professional background and perceived privacy risk on initial trust in autonomous vehicles, highlighting the differences between professionals and non-professionals in the formation of initial trust. The findings further suggest that designers of autonomous driving systems should adopt more targeted trust calibration strategies to address the differential responses of professionals and non-professionals.

articles.title: “It’s a Trap!” – Faking in multidimensional forced choice response formats: Latent change score modeling of faking across MFC and RS formats
articles.authors: Alexander Leonard Schünemann; Matthias Ziegler
articles.abstract: The present study examines the faking susceptibility of rating scale (RS) and multidimensional forced-choice (MFC) questionnaires in high-stakes as opposed to low-stakes assessment contexts. Following the transformation of a situation-based Big Five questionnaire (B5PS; Ziegler, 2014) from a RS into an MFC response format and a successful Thurstonian IRT (TIRT) calibration, the MFC version was evaluated in a lab-simulated high-stakes assessment situation. The vulnerability of MFC and RS formats to intentional response distortion was assessed in a within-subject design (N = 187). Participants were instructed to assume the role of applicants in a fictitious job application process and were encouraged to fake their responses on both the MFC questionnaire and a standard RS Big Five inventory (BIF-2; Danner et al., 2016). In the faking condition, participants were asked to present themselves as ideal candidates according to a predefined job requirement profile, targeting selected Big Five domains, namely Conscientiousness, Emotional Stability, and Agreeableness. Latent Change Score Modelling (LCSM) was then applied to model faking behavior at the individual level across honest and faking conditions for both response formats. Analyses revealed a strong faking effect in the RS format, whereas the LCSM did not fit the MFC data for any Big Five domain except Agreeableness (A). Results demonstrate the reduced faking susceptibility of the MFC format relative to the RS format. A coherent faking factor did not emerge in the MFC format, while homogeneous faking behavior was evident in the RS format. These findings are discussed in relation to current empirical evidence on the susceptibility of MFC formats to faking in high-stakes assessment situations, with reference to low-stakes assessment contexts. The specific role of Emotional Stability (N) in the faking susceptibility of MFC questionnaires is highlighted, corroborating findings from previous research.

articles.title: The EFL-PAI: Development and validation of a group-administered assessment of phonological abilities in Grade 4
articles.authors: Natalja Ulrich; Nils Jaekel; Michael Schurig; Steinlen Anja; Thorsten Piske
articles.abstract:
Phonological abilities (PAs), encompassing word and phonological awareness, remain crucial throughout literacy development and are robust predictors of reading achievement in first and second languages. Although numerous PAs instruments exist, most focus on the first language, are not publicly available, or require individual administration.
The present study aimed to develop and validate a reliable group-administered assessment of PAs for English as a foreign language, resulting in the English as a Foreign Language Phonological Abilities Instrument (EFL-PAI).
The EFL- PAI comprises 29 items across five subtests targeting word, rhyme, syllable, and phoneme awareness. Data from 389 learners in Finland and Norway were analysed using descriptive statistics and Rasch models to examine item functioning, reliability, and construct validity.
The results indicate that the instrument provides a reliable measure of PAs in EFL learners and shows evidence of validity. It allows evaluation of individual differences, as well as making predictions about EFL reading skills

articles.title: Cognition About Cognition: Do Scales From Different Fields Assess Metacognition Alike?
articles.authors: Sebastian Bürgler; Renato Frey
articles.abstract: Metacognition is a construct of long-lasting interest in multiple fields of research. Yet, exchange between fields has been limited, leaving it an open question to what extent this construct can be conceptualized as a general cognitive entity. We thus implemented a cross-disciplinary analysis investigating if self-report scales from four fields tap into the same underlying construct and give rise to a general factor of metacognition (M). In a preregistered online study (N = 661) and utilizing an analytical approach to mitigate overfitting, a systematic model comparison showed that a bifactor model including a general factor of metacognition performed best. This general factor explained 61% of the systematic variance, suggesting that there exists an important general component of metacognition. We will discuss how the different subscales of the four scales relate to one another and to M, elaborate on a potential jingle-fallacy in metacognition research, and give recommendations on which subscales to use to best tap into M. In sum, our integrative approach contributes to a better understanding of metacognition and how to best measure it.

articles.title: Strong alliance, weak conclusions: Comment on Goodwin et al. (2026) “The role of therapeutic alliance in psilocybin treatment for treatment-resistant depression”
articles.authors: Max Wolff; Samuli Kangaslampi; Richard Zeifman; Moritz Spangemacher
articles.abstract: This commentary critically examines the interpretation and analytic choices in Goodwin and colleagues’ recent analysis of therapeutic alliance in psilocybin treatment for treatment-resistant depression. While the authors conclude that alliance did not meaningfully contribute to treatment efficacy, we argue that this interpretation is not supported by the reported results, which are, in addition, shaped by methodological decisions that obscure relevant effects. By contextualizing the observed associations, clarifying the logic of mediation analysis, and pointing out methodological weaknesses, we show that the available evidence is more consistent with a meaningful role of therapeutic alliance in shaping both the psychedelic experience and clinical outcomes. Furthermore, we highlight unexplained deviations from the study protocol that warrant scrutiny. These concerns underscore the importance of accurately characterizing psychological and contextual factors in psychedelic treatment research and calls for more comprehensive and transparent analyses of psychotherapeutic processes.

articles.title: Effects of Action Type on the Attentional Boost Effect
articles.authors: Xiangyu Li; Kanji Tanaka
articles.abstract: The attentional boost effect (ABE) refers to the enhancement of memory for concurrently presented information when a target is detected during a dual task. Although ABE reliably emerges with simple motor responses, such as button presses (i.e., taps), it remains unclear whether it generalizes to actions with higher cognitive demands, such as swipe actions. The present study investigated how action type (tap vs. swipe) and the visuospatial attention associated with each action task influence the ABE. Across three experiments, participants performed a Go/No-go task while encoding background images, followed by a recognition test. In the tap condition, participants pressed a key in response to Go cues, whereas in the swipe condition, they swiped in the direction indicated by the target. In Experiment 1, potential targets appeared at eight spatially distributed locations. The Result showed that the ABE emerged in the tap condition but not in the swipe condition. In Experiments 2 and 3, targets were presented at the center of the screen, and ABEs were observed for both actions. Reaction times were consistently shorter for taps than for swipes. These findings suggest that action-related cognitive demands do not attenuate the ABE; rather, visuospatial attention linked to specific action environments may modulate its magnitude.

articles.title: Metacognitive Confidence, AI Familiarity, and Heuristic Judgment in AI-Generated Text Attribution: An Exploratory Study Among Undergraduate Students
articles.authors: Zannatul Ferdous
articles.abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly integrated into educational and everyday contexts, raising important questions about how individuals evaluate AI-generated content. This study examined the relationships between self-reported AI familiarity, metacognitive confidence, and judgment performance in AI-generated text attribution among undergraduate students. An exploratory cross-sectional design was employed with 66 participants who completed a forced-choice sentence judgment task consisting of 20 items, all generated using ChatGPT. After each item, participants also reported their confidence in their judgments, and self-reported AI familiarity was assessed using a Likert-type scale.
Results indicated that judgment performance was relatively low (M = .43, SD = .15), while metacognitive confidence was comparatively high (M = 3.84, SD = .87). Correlation analyses revealed a weak negative relationship between AI familiarity and judgment performance, a moderate positive relationship between AI familiarity and confidence, and a weak negative relationship between confidence and judgment performance. These findings suggest a dissociation between subjective confidence and actual performance in AI-text attribution tasks. Qualitative responses further indicated that participants frequently relied on heuristic cues, particularly perceived formality and linguistic style, when evaluating text. Overall, the findings suggest that perceived familiarity with AI systems is more strongly associated with increased confidence than with improved evaluative performance. The study contributes to emerging literature on AI literacy and metacognition by highlighting limitations in judgment calibration and the role of heuristic processing in AI-generated text evaluation.

articles.title: The Cost of Belonging—and Leaving:  Structure, Predictors, and Mental Health Outcomes of Costly Religious Exits
articles.authors: David Bridger Lisonbee; Yehudis Keller; Julie J. Exline; Daryl R. Van Tongeren; Sam Hardy
articles.abstract: Leaving a high-demand religion is often psychologically costly, yet most research focuses on simple outcomes rather than the structure and mechanisms of costly exit experiences. This study examined mental health outcomes associated with high-cost religious exits. Participants (N = 1221) had left the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS; n = 426), ultra-Orthodox Judaism (n = 292), fundamentalist Evangelical Christian (n = 279), and moderate/liberal Christian traditions (n = 209). Using a dimensional-categorical spectrum approach, results supported a single dimension of costly experiences composed of summed costs of leaving, negative exit experiences, spiritual harms and abuses, gender discrimination, religious/spiritual struggles, and demonic spiritual struggles. Higher costliness was predicted by religion of origin, gender, and sexual orientation. More intense costly experiences were associated with both higher PTSD symptoms and greater posttraumatic growth (PTG). Religious background moderated both outcomes: costly experiences predicted the highest PTSD among ultra-Orthodox Jewish individuals and was also elevated among LDS and fundamentalist evangelical participants, while ultra-Orthodox Jewish and fundamentalist evangelical participants reported less growth from their costly experiences. Gender moderated the relationship between costly experiences and PTG but not PTSD, with women experiencing less growth from their costly experiences. Sexual minority status moderated the relationship between costly experiences and PTSD, but not PTG. Overall, results suggest that the costly experiences associated with deidentification from high-demand religions begin not at exit, but during membership, and are strongly linked to both PTSD symptoms and PTG — with demographic moderators.

articles.title: Temporal Dynamics of Selective Enumeration for a Small Number of Emotional Faces
articles.authors: Kei Kobayashi; Tetsuko Kasai
articles.abstract: Face-to-face communication with a small number of others requires perceiving each person’s facial expressions, as emotional states often vary across people. Although multiple-object individuation, defined as the representation of distinct entities in a scene, has been investigated for geometric figures using event-related potentials (ERPs) during enumeration tasks, the extent to which these processes apply to social entities remains largely unclear. The present study aimed to examine the temporal dynamics of selective enumeration of emotional faces in a small group context. Thirty participants viewed four faces (two per hemifield) for 200 ms and enumerated zero, one, or two happy faces in a cued target hemifield, with the remaining faces displaying neutral expressions. Behavioral results confirmed accurate target enumeration. Lateralized ERPs relative to the cued target hemifield, specifically the P1 posterior contralateral (90–150 ms poststimulus onset), N2 posterior contralateral (250–300 ms poststimulus), and contralateral delay activity (400–600 ms), were all modulated by target numerosity. These findings suggest that individuated representations of up to two happy faces were formed during early-to-intermediate processing stages and subsequently maintained in working memory. Overall, the results provide an initial step toward understanding the neurocognitive mechanisms underlying group-member individuation, which may support effective interactions in small groups.

articles.title: The Truly Horrific Face of Masculinity: Unravelling the Phenomenon of a Global Online Rape Academy
articles.authors: Andreas Walther; Katharina Haslacher
articles.abstract: This review elucidates the phenomenon of a global online rape academy through the lens of the psychology of men and masculinities. Triggered by recent investigative reports documenting online communities in which men exchanged strategies for sedating, sexually assaulting, recording, and distributing abuse against unconscious women, the article aims to move beyond explanations focused solely on individual pathology and instead explore broader psychosocial, cultural, and technological mechanisms that may contribute to such violence. From the outset of the Confluence Model of Sexual Aggression and drawing on the Gender Role Strain Paradigm (GRSP) and Precarious Manhood Theory (PMT), the article discusses how rigid masculinity norms emphasizing dominance, emotional restriction, entitlement, sexual conquest, and control over women may contribute to the normalization and reinforcement of misogynistic violence within digitally connected peer environments. Additional concepts, including masculine sexual entitlement, narcissistic entitlement, externalizing forms of male distress, as well as technology acceptance frameworks, are integrated to illustrate how online platforms can facilitate anonymity, group validation, tactical coordination, and the amplification of violent masculine performances. The article further highlights important explanatory aspects differing from the Confluence Model of Sexual Aggression and argues that these online networks represent not merely isolated deviance, but socially reinforced ecosystems in which violence becomes normalized, rewarded, and collectively organized. At the same time, the paper emphasizes that masculinity itself is not inherently violent and that the overwhelming majority of men do not engage in sexual violence. Understanding the psychological and sociocultural mechanisms contributing to such extreme forms of abuse may nevertheless help improve prevention, victim protection, digital platform regulation, and international criminal prosecution, while fostering healthier and less harmful models of masculinity.

articles.title: Build Better Relationships: Positive Empathy and Capitalization
articles.authors: Gregory John Depow; Amie Michelle Gordon; Yumeng Gu; Christopher Oveis
articles.abstract: When individuals share positive events with others, they can experience benefits beyond the positive event itself, a phenomenon known as capitalization. However, the perceived quality of the responder’s reaction to a capitalization attempt shapes the impact it will have for the capitalizer. Here, we examined how different dimensions of empathy (e.g., compassion, perspective taking, emotion sharing) in listeners predict perceived responses to capitalization attempts by sharers. We recruited 103 stranger dyads (N = 206) who engaged in naturalistic conversations about a meaningful positive event. Participants watched video recordings of their interactions and continuously rated their own and their partner's emotions using a sliding scale. Results revealed that co-experienced positive affect—moments when both the listener and sharer simultaneously experienced positive emotions—was a key predictor of perceived responses to capitalization attempts.
