articles.title: Building language: Mean Length of Utterance, Lexical Growth, and Grammatical Accuracy in Spanish-speaking Monolingual Children
articles.authors: Alejandra Auza; Maria Kapantzoglou; Chiharu Murata; Reinaldo Cabrera Pérez
articles.abstract:
Purpose: This study established age-based reference benchmarks for grammaticality and lexical productivity in monolingual Spanish-speaking children using narrative retell tasks. Developmental changes in Mean Length of Utterance in words (MLUw), Total Number of Words (TNW), Percentage of Grammatical Utterances (PGU), and Moving Average Type-Token Ratio (MATTR) were examined in children aged 3;0–6;11 years.
Method: A cross-sectional quantitative design was used with 819 monolingual Spanish-speaking children recruited from mainstream educational settings. Narrative retell tasks elicited spontaneous language samples, from which MLUw, TNW, PGU, and MATTR were derived. Age-based descriptive and percentile analyses were conducted.
Results: Results showed age-related increases in lexical productivity and grammatical accuracy. TNW increased from 105 words at age 3 to 180 words at age 6. PGU improved from 75% at age 3 to approximately 88–90% at ages 4–6. MATTR remained relatively stable across age groups, ranging from 0.46 to 0.48.
Conclusion: Findings support conceptualising language performance as a developmental continuum and demonstrate the diagnostic utility of percentile-based interpretation. This study provides the first comprehensive reference benchmarks for narrative language measures in monolingual Spanish-speaking children and offers clinically relevant tools for more precise assessment in Spanish-speaking contexts.

articles.title: An unusual cycle of literary translations reveals distinct mechanisms of cultural evolution
articles.authors: Ellis Cain; Elizabeth Geballe; Tyler Marghetis
articles.abstract: How do stories change as they cross linguistic and cultural boundaries? Many theories of cultural evolution emphasize the influence of repeated transmission, but in real-world cross-cultural dissemination, transmission is typically confounded with large-scale changes in cultural context. This makes it difficult to identify mechanisms of cultural stability and change. Here, to unravel this theoretical tangle, we leverage an unusual translation cycle: a story first published in English was translated into Russian by novelist Leo Tolstoy, misattributed to Tolstoy, translated into French, and finally translated back into English --- with the first and final versions published in the same language, city (Chicago, USA), and time period (mid-1890s). This unusual 'translation cycle' offers a unique opportunity to separate the cumulative effects of iterated transmission from the influences of the broader cultural context. Using a novel computational method that represents stories as high-dimensional semantic trajectories, we show that each translation introduced cumulative distortions. Strikingly, when the story was translated back into its original cultural context, its semantic trajectory shifted toward the original version, despite the final translator having no knowledge of the original, consistent with a regularizing influence of the shared cultural context. Our findings reveal how the broader cultural context can act as a stabilizing force in cultural evolution, constraining variation introduced by repeated transmission.

articles.title: The Development of Social Reasoning About Shared Sleep Arrangements
articles.authors: Rodney Tompkins; Adena Schachner
articles.abstract: Shared sleep arrangements, especially children’s, often occur in proximity to family and other caregivers. We investigated whether sleep-sharing serves as a reliable cue to familial and caregiving relationships, even in childhood. Two preregistered studies with U.S. 5–7-year-olds (N = 168) and adults (N = 168) show people possess systematic intuitions about the social meaning of shared sleep arrangements. In Study 1, both age groups robustly expected that a child character sleep-shared with family over non-family (siblings over friends; parents over teachers), while expecting equal toy-sharing. In Study 2, both age groups predicted that a child’s chosen sleep-sharing (vs. toy-sharing) partner would provide physical comfort (care) when the child became upset. Adults privileged sleep-sharing over another intimate interaction––saliva-sharing––as diagnostic of a caregiving relationship. From early childhood, people use shared sleep arrangements as a powerful signal of intimate relationships, supporting their understanding of the social landscape.

articles.title: The Structure of Intersectional Educational Inequality Over Two Decades
articles.authors: Lisa Bardach; Mia Herkommer; Lena Keller; Nicolas Hübner
articles.abstract: Educational inequalities—systematic differences in achievement between social groups— persist worldwide. Yet long-term evidence on how such inequalities unfold at intersections of multiple social group categories (e.g., gender, socioeconomic status, immigrant background) remains limited. Using multilevel intersectional models adapted from epidemiology, we analyzed two decades of assessment data in reading, mathematics, and science (over 537,000 students from Australia, Brazil, Finland, Germany, Hong Kong, Italy, Israel, and the United States). In most countries, additive effects of gender, socioeconomic status, and immigrant background accounted for most between-group structuring in intersectional inequality. Intersection-specific components were mostly small but persistent. Large country-specific gaps separated the highest- and lowest-performing intersectional groups, reaching over 10 years of learning. We highlighted intersections with pronounced (dis)advantages, underscoring the need to address compounded disparities. Overall, these findings clarify the structure of intersectional educational inequality across countries and over time.

articles.title: The relation between processing speed and working memory capacity: No difference across varying procedural working memory demands
articles.authors: Isabel Courage; Gidon T. Frischkorn
articles.abstract: The binding hypothesis has been proposed as a potential explanation of the observed relation between processing speed (PS) and working memory capacity (WMC). According to this hypothesis, PS and WMC are correlated as both demand WM maintenance. However, past studies showed mixed results for the binding hypothesis as an explanation. Here, we extended this line of work by examining whether correlations between individuals’ PS and WMC are strengthened when procedural WM demands on reconfiguration and protection against proactive interference in PS tasks are high. We compared correlations when high procedural WM demands are afforded as stimulus-response mapping change throughout PS tasks relative to consistent mappings with only one set of mappings throughout PS tasks. Yet, our results show similar correlations between PS and WMC when higher procedural WM demands are implemented questioning whether the binding hypothesis can serve as an explanation for the relation between PS and WMC. However, performance in PS tasks with changing and consistent mapping was highly correlated indicating that factors or processes unaffected by consistencies drive the PS-WMC relation. Future studies should examine whether cue-stimulus intervals, set sizes, or methods to test the binding hypothesis could have mitigated procedural WM demands in PS tasks accounting for the lack of correlational differences.

articles.title: The Coding Brain: P600 Effects Elicited by the Visual Programming Language ScratchJr
articles.authors: Emily Nadler; Jason Geller; Ryan Rhodes; Marina Bers
articles.abstract:
In recent years, there is a growing emphasis on early childhood coding education to equip
students with essential skills for an increasingly technical society. While emerging research has
investigated how text-based programming languages are processed in the adult brain, the neural
mechanisms underlying early childhood visual programming languages like ScratchJr remain
largely unexplored. This preregistered study examined the cognitive basis of ScratchJr
processing, with two primary research aims: 1. Investigate whether ScratchJr elicits neural
signatures comparable to natural languages and other rule-based processes (specifically N400
and semantic P600 responses) and 2. Explore how ScratchJr competence modulates these effects.
We recorded event-related potentials (ERPs) from 40 (N = 40) adults while they completed a
semantic congruency task using ScratchJr stimuli. Neural responses were analyzed in time bins
associated with N400 and P600 components, and ScratchJr competence was included as a
potential factor to modulate these results. Results revealed a P600 response to semantic
violations, with no modulation by ScratchJr competence. These findings contribute to a growing
body of literature on the processing of programming languages and support the notion that
ScratchJr processing evokes neural mechanisms related to structured processes such as natural
language.

articles.title: A triadic model of bilingual shared reading:  Contributions of the book, parent, and child in shaping dual language use
articles.authors: Marissa Castellana; Christine Potter; Viridiana L. Benitez
articles.abstract: This study proposes and tests a triadic model of shared reading in bilingual contexts. Sixty-two Spanish-English bilingual parents (92% mothers, 88% Latino) and children (M=3.86 years, 51.6% male) read an English-only Monolingual book and bilingual Codeswitching book with Spanish words embedded in English text. Both parents and children produced more Spanish and codeswitching when sharing Codeswitching vs. Monolingual books, and dyads’ Spanish use was positively correlated while reading both books. These findings reveal that books, parents, and children all make meaningful contributions to the learning opportunities available during shared reading, consistent with the proposed triadic model. This framework can inform a deeper understanding of shared reading interactions that include diverse language experiences and environments.

articles.title: Sensitivity Analysis of Computational Models in Psychology: A Tutorial
articles.authors: Valeria Epelbaum; Sophie Engels; Jill de Ron; Jonas M B Haslbeck; Shirley B Wang; Derek de Beurs; Denny Borsboom; Valeria Krzhizhanovskaya
articles.abstract: There has been an increasing interest in formal models in psychology, allowing researchers to betterunderstand and intervene on psychological systems. However, the methods and tools for understandingand analysing these models are rarely used in psychological research. This is not necessarily due to a lackof methodology, but rather due to them not being familiar in this field. This paper introduces one suchmethod from computational science that can be used to determine the influence of model parameterson an output variable: (global) Sensitivity Analysis (SA). We first discuss the main goals of an SA andpotential applications, such as determining which parameters are the most relevant for causing a changein a chosen model output, such as suicidal thoughts or frequency of panic attacks. We then presenttwo methods that can be used to conduct an SA: the variance-based Sobol SA and the density-basedPAWN. Both of them are demonstrated using an existing computational model in a tutorial-like fashion,provided by (annotated) code in R. The results are connected to empirical literature and implicationsfor model evaluations are discussed. Lastly, we aggregate the entire process into a workflow and codepipeline that can be easily applied to other models. Although this paper only demonstrates the basicapplication of SA, further extensions and purposes, such as model calibration, are discussed.

articles.title: The Behavioral Science Conference Presentations Database
articles.authors: Joseph Siev
articles.abstract: Behavioral scientists give thousands of conference talks each year, and these presentations are the field's fastest-circulating record of active research. Yet they have not been systematically indexed. I introduce the Behavioral Science Conference Presentations Database, a public, free-to-use archive that currently spans 73,248 presentations across 21 conference series and 144 individual meetings from 2015 to 2026, with seven search modes and landing pages for each conference, session, topic, and author. The index is at https://www.joesiev.com/conferences. Three descriptive findings illustrate its utility. I first explore three behavioral subfields—consumer research (ACR), social psychology (SPSP), and organizational behavior (AOM-OB)—and document their shifting topic profiles between 2015 and 2025, including the explosion of AI-related papers presented at these meetings. I find that this work has predominantly treated AI as a psychological stimulus, but increasingly uses it as a research tool or method and even as a subject of psychological study itself. I then zoom in on marketing, exploring how topic shares at ACR have shifted between psychological process, marketplace/consumption, social issues, and digital technology. These findings show how research attention shifts within and across subfields over time, highlighting the meta-scientific value of conference programs.

articles.title: Altered brain functional connectivity linked to memory suppression in individuals with alcohol use disorder
articles.authors: Rui Rodrigues; Alberto Crego; Alberto J. González-Villar; Adriana Sampaio; Eduardo López-Caneda
articles.abstract: Individuals are able to voluntarily suppress unwanted thoughts or memories, a phenomenon commonly referred to as memory suppression or suppression-induced forgetting. Although alcohol use disorder (AUD) has been associated with impairments in inhibitory control, episodic memory, and heightened salience of alcohol-related cues, the neural mechanisms underlying the suppression of alcohol-related memories remain poorly understood. The present study aimed to investigate behavioral and neurofunctional correlates of memory inhibition in abstinent individuals with AUD using an adapted Think/No-Think Alcohol paradigm combined with task-based functional connectivity analyses. Forty-one participants (26 abstinent individuals with AUD; 15 controls) completed encoding, Think/No-Think (during functional magnetic resonance imaging) and retrieval phases. Individuals with AUD showed reduced encoding and overall recall performance, while suppression-induced forgetting was comparable between groups. At the neural level, during attempts to suppress alcohol-related memories, AUD participants exhibited increased connectivity between reward-related subcortical regions and prefrontal, perceptual, and interoceptive cortices, suggesting increased engagement of salience and reward systems during suppression demands, whereas controls preferentially recruited classic top-down memory control circuitry. In contrast, during suppression of non-alcohol memories, AUD participants showed stronger fronto-posterior coupling than controls, indicating that suppression-related control mechanisms remained recruitable but were engaged in a stimulus-dependent manner, alongside evidence of generalized salience-related modulation of perceptual processing. Additionally, during the recall of alcohol-related stimuli, the AUD group showed stronger coupling within salience and valuation circuitry, while controls engaged goal-directed and perceptual imagery systems. Within the AUD group, craving, particularly compulsivity, was associated with greater recall of suppressed alcohol-related memories and with altered functional connectivity in regulatory and salience networks. Together, the findings suggest that memory control in AUD is characterized not by overt behavioral suppression failure but by salience-biased redistribution of neural recruitment. These results highlight neural-level vulnerability in memory regulation that may contribute to craving persistence and relapse risk, providing potential targets for neurocognitive interventions aimed at strengthening memory control in individuals with AUD.

articles.title: A Beta Way: A Tutorial on Bayesian Beta Regression for Psychological Research
articles.authors: Jason Geller; Robert Kubinec; Chelsea Parlett; Matti Vuorre
articles.abstract: Rates, percentages, and proportions are common outcomes in psychology and the social sciences. These outcomes are often analyzed using models that assume normality, but this practice overlooks important features of the data, such as their natural bounds at 0 and 1. As a result, estimates can become distorted. In contrast, treating such outcomes as Beta-distributed respects these limits and can yield more accurate estimates. Despite these advantages, the use of Beta models in applied research remains limited. Our goal is to provide researchers with practical guidance for adopting Beta regression models, illustrated with an example drawn from the psychological literature. We begin by introducing the Beta distribution and Beta regression, emphasizing key components and assumptions. Next, using data from a learning and memory study, we demonstrate how to fit a Beta regression model in R with the Bayesian package `brms` and how to interpret results on the response scale. We also discuss model extensions, including zero-inflated, zero- and one-inflated, and ordered Beta models. Basic familarity with regression modeling and R is assumed. To promote wider adoption of these methods, we provide detailed code and materials at https://github.com/jgeller112/Beta_regression_tutorial.

articles.title: Dissociating Sensory and Decision Processes in Tasting: Intensity Affects Sensitivity, while Expectancies and Feedback Interact, Affecting Decision Criteria
articles.authors: Manuel Miguel Ramos-Alvarez; Teresa L. Martín-Guerrero; Juan M Rosas
articles.abstract: The aim of this study was to examine the influence of feedback on the dynamics of sensory and decision processes in a tasting task involving mixtures of salty and sour basic tastes. The Double Dissociation Additive Test within the Signal Detection Theory framework was applied to assess the effects of manipulating intensity, signal-noise base rates, and feedback on sensitivity and decision-making processes. Participants’ discrimination improved as salt concentration increased, regardless of induced bias or feedback presence; however, feedback led to a general increase in sensitivity. Both signal-noise stimulus base rates and feedback influenced the decision-making process. When feedback was present, detection judgments aligned more closely with actual signal-noise stimulus base rates. However, in the absence of feedback, the relationship between base rates and response bias reversed. Implications for research in sensory evaluation and perceptual learning are discussed.

articles.title: Measuring Behavioral Avoidance in Misophonia: Development and Validation of the Misophonic Behavioral Avoidance Test (M-BAT)
articles.authors: Nico Remmert; Jane Gregory; Sewon Oh; Svetlana Shinkareva; Silia Vitoratou; Steffi Pohl
articles.abstract: Avoidance is central in selective sound intolerance (misophonia), yet lacks objective behavioral assessment. We present the Misophonic Behavioral Avoidance Test (M-BAT), a standardized computerized task with two subtests to measure reactive (sound endurance) and anticipating avoidance tendency via behavioral indicators and response times (RTs). Different than most behavioral avoidance tests, the M-BAT can be easily applied online to large samples. In a validation study on N = 381 adults with misophonic symptoms, we fitted Bayesian Item Response Theory (IRT) models to endurance times, avoidance indicators and RTs to evaluate reliability, measurement invariance (MI), and several facets of validity. Unidimensional models fitted subtests well with high reliability (ρEAP ≈ .88/.94), and MI across gender, misophonia severity and age was broadly supported, despite localized differential item functioning. Sound endurance and anticipating avoidance tendency were moderately related (r = -.30) and showed theory-consistent, moderate associations with self-reported misophonic symptoms including avoidance, distinguished clinical from subclinical misophonia groups, and predicted everyday behavior (daily escape and prevention). We found little evidence that contextual factors, such as the stimuli presentation or the individuality of stimuli influenced test responses, however, findings demonstrated influences due to participants high test motivation. Challenges in establishing discriminant, internal, and incremental validity remain. Overall, the M-BAT provides reliable, easily applicable, and objective behavioral measures that complement questionnaires and psychoacoustic ratings, supporting clinical research and treatment evaluation.

articles.title: AMPT : A Tool for Personalizing Math Learning with Generative AI
articles.authors: Kole Andreas Norberg; April Murphy; Steve Ritter
articles.abstract: We developed the AI Math Personalization Tool (AMPT) to explore how personalization and agency over the contexts in math story problems can affect student learning and motivation for mathematics. AMPT uses a conversational interface to learn about students’ interests before creating a math story problem targeting those interests. Feedback gathered through co-design sessions with AMPT provided insight into areas where agency over the story of the problem was particularly important to students (e.g., naming characters) as well as the contextual topics students find interesting. Further, following AMPT sessions, students’ sense of belonging in mathematics increased. By leveraging generative AI to support personalization at scale, AMPT provides a framework for investigating how agency and representation relate to student engagement in mathematics. These insights have broader implications for the design of AI-driven learning tools and the future of personalized education.

articles.title: A Practical Unified Framework for Evaluating Psychological Theories and Observational Research Practices
articles.authors: Peter Hilpert; Matthew J Vowels
articles.abstract:
In psychology, knowledge advances primarily through theory development and empirical testing within a dominant framework: theory, hypothesis, design, measurement, modeling, and interpretation. Although rigorous execution is assumed to yield valid insights, meta-scientific critiques reveal profound shortcomings. This article addresses these by (1) identifying seven core deficiencies in theory development; (2) delineating seven critical flaws in observational empirical testing; and (3) introducing three coherence dimensions (conceptual, causal, technical) across the pipeline as an integrative requirement. We synthesize these previously fragmented critiques into a novel, unified evaluative framework consisting of 14 explicit evaluation criteria plus three coherence dimensions. For each component we derive concrete, manuscript-level assessment criteria that can be applied at the sentence and section level. We demonstrate the framework’s practical utility by applying it to two published empirical papers, revealing detectable issues even in high-quality work. 
Compounded with well-documented problems such as low statistical power (~36%) and replication rates of 30–50%, these upstream deficiencies likely render a substantial proportion of published findings fragile or uninterpretable. Importantly, these flaws lie upstream of replication: while replication is essential for assessing reproducibility and can motivate theory revision, it cannot remedy deeper theoretical and methodological weaknesses and may, in some cases, reinforce flawed conclusions. By providing a practical tool for structured assessment and reform, this framework aims to strengthen theory construction, empirical rigor, and cumulative progress in psychology and related social sciences.

articles.title: Stealth Assessments in Technology-Rich Learning Environments:  A Systematic Review of Implementation, Modeling, and Psychometric Quality
articles.authors: Seyedahmad Rahimi; Salah Esmaeiligoujar; Deniz Ercan; Valerie Shute
articles.abstract: Advances in digital technologies, data logging, and computational modeling increasingly enable unobtrusive, continuous assessment as learners interact with technology-rich environments. Stealth assessment reflects this shift by embedding evidence-based assessment within learning activities to support real-time inference, feedback, and personalization. Following PRISMA guidelines, this systematic review synthesized 161 publications representing 164 study entries published between 2004 and 2026 to examine: (1) where and how stealth assessment has been implemented across environments, populations, competencies, and purposes; and (2) how studies modeled evidence identification and accumulation in relation to psychometric quality, fidelity to evidence-centered design, and validity evidence. Results show expansion beyond games into simulations, intelligent tutoring systems, virtual reality, and applied domains, but continued concentration in K–12 contexts and cognitive or disciplinary competencies. Modeling sophistication varied widely but was not associated with stronger psychometric quality reporting or greater fidelity. Validity evidence was commonly reported, whereas reliability and fairness were rarely addressed.

articles.title: When ‘Right Now’ Isn’t ‘Right Now’: The Elasticity of the Moment(ary) in ESM Studies
articles.authors: Dominique Maciejewski; Leonie Schorrlepp; Leonie Cloos; Zachary Daniel Cohen; M. Annelise Blanchard; Melissa Miléna De Smet; Marie Stadel
articles.abstract: Recently, Eisele and colleagues (2025) published the ESM-Q, a set of quality criteria for developing and evaluating Experience Sampling Method (ESM) items. While we agree with one of their core recommendations to specify the timeframe an item refers to (e.g., “Right now, I feel happy”), we comment here on how this may be less straightforward than implicitly assumed by ESM researchers. Drawing on our own ESM studies, in which we collected qualitative data from participants, we show that the popular momentary timeframe “right now” is interpreted by participants in different ways, often covering not only the moment just before the assessment but drawing on past, present and anticipated future times. We outline how differences in interpretations can be explained by the linguistic ambiguity of the time adverbs that we use to signify timeframes, as well as by participants’ individual experience of time. We close the commentary with implications on the severity of these differences in interpretation, and provide some concrete recommendations for ESM researchers.

articles.title: Workload and Feedback Immediacy Shape Willingness to Offload Working Memory to AI: Neural evidence
articles.authors: Tobias Feldmann-Wüstefeld; Eva Wiese
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study examined how workload and feedback timing influence cognitive offloading to AI systems, using neural measures to track how operators allocate working memory resources between internal processing and external support.

Background: Effective human–AI teaming depends on calibrated trust and appropriate cognitive offloading. However, feedback timing may influence how reliably system performance is learned and how operators distribute cognitive resources during interaction.

Method: In two experiments, participants performed a change-detection task alone (solo blocks) or alongside an AI monitoring half the visual field (dyad blocks). Workload was manipulated within, feedback immediacy (block-wise vs. trial-wise) between participants. The contralateral delay activity (CDA) served as a neural index of working memory maintenance and cognitive offloading.

Results: Dyad blocks produced reduced CDA amplitudes relative to solo blocks, consistent with incomplete offloading to the AI. When feedback was non-immediate (block-wise; Experiment 1), solo-dyad CDA differences were identical under low and high workload, indicating that offloading was not modulated by task demands. When feedback was immediate (trial-wise; Experiment 2), self-reported trust was increased and solo-dyad CDA differences were pronounced under low workload but attenuated under high workload. This indicates that offloading increased specifically when immediate feedback coincided with high task demands, together with higher attitudinal trust.

Conclusion: Offloading increased specifically when immediate feedback and high workload coincided. Neural measures captured internal monitoring processes invisible to behavioral measures alone.

Application: AI systems providing timely, transparent feedback may support calibrated trust and promote efficient cognitive resource allocation in human–AI teaming environment.

articles.title: Sensitive Periods for and Accumulation of Childhood Adversity on Adolescent Development in Ghana
articles.authors: Hang Do; Noelle Suntheimer; Andrew Smith; Erin C. Dunn; Elisabetta Aurino; Sharon Wolf
articles.abstract: Childhood adversity can have profound consequences on adolescents’ cognitive and social-emotional development, yet whether the developmental timing or the accumulation of exposure to these experiences over time is most predictive of unfavorable later-life outcomes remains unclear. This study draws on a longitudinal sample in peri-urban Ghana (N = 1,921; 50.4% girls, all Ghanaian nationals) to examine how children’s exposure to five adversities at ages 5, 6, 7, 8, and 11 predicts outcomes at age 13. We employed the Structured Life Course Modeling Approach (SLCMA) to test whether sensitive periods or accumulation life-course hypotheses most strongly predicted and accounted for variation in adolescent outcomes. Models specifying a sensitive period were better-fitting than accumulation models in predicting adolescent outcomes: physical aggression and household food insecurity at age 11 were the most predictive of lower cognitive and social-emotional outcomes, and household poverty at age 8 was most predictive of lower emotion regulation. Sensitive periods at ages 5 and 6 for low cognitive stimulation and accumulation of household poverty were the most predictive of adolescents’ cognitive abilities. Findings suggest that social protection and educational programs may be most effective when tailored to the types, sensitive periods, or accumulation of adversities experienced across childhood.

articles.title: Reduced working memory offloading to unreliable artificial intelligence: Evidence from the contralateral delay activity
articles.authors: Tobias Feldmann-Wüstefeld; Marian-David Wolff; Eva Wiese
articles.abstract: Efficient collaboration between humans and artificial intelligence (AI) systems requires a dynamic allocation of cognitive resources. Individuals must decide whether to maintain information internally or offload cognitive processing to artificial systems. Although such decisions depend critically on trust, it remains a challenge how to measure trust: existing approaches rely primarily on self-report or behavioral proxies that are indirect and often confounded by task demands. Here we tested whether the contralateral delay activity (CDA), a well-established neural marker of working memory storage, can serve as an implicit neural indicator of trust-dependent cognitive offloading. Human participants (both sexes) performed a lateralized change-detection task while electroencephalography (EEG) was recorded. Working memory load (low, high) and interaction type (solo, reliable, unreliable AI partner) were manipulated using a repeated measures design. Participants were instructed that the AI would monitor one visual hemifield while they monitored the other, allowing them to offload memory demands depending on their trust in the AI. CDA amplitude varied systematically as a function of AI reliability and memory load. Under low working memory load, working with the reliable AI elicited larger CDA amplitudes than working with the unreliable AI, consistent with greater cognitive offloading to the automated partner. However, this effect disappeared under high memory load, suggesting that increased cognitive demands limit the extent of offloading. These findings demonstrate that the CDA provides a continuous neural index of how individuals allocate cognitive resources when interacting with artificial systems, offering a promising approach to measuring trust-related cognitive processes in human-AI collaboration.

articles.title: Opening the Black Box of Scientific Practices – a Systematic Video Development Framework Integrating Scientific Reasoning Skills and Insights into Authentic Research Processes
articles.authors: Katharina Düsing; Vanessa van den Bogaert; Miriam Brandt; Till Bruckermann; Hannah Greving; Ute Harms; Joachim Kimmerle; Daniel Lewanzik; Anke Schumann; Julia Cathérine Thomas
articles.abstract: Understanding scientific practices is fundamental to our modern society and to science education. Concepts such as Scientific Reasoning (SR) and Nature of Science (NOS) are important to operationalize different aspects of these practices. Educational policy organizations and international standards emphasize the importance of insights into the activities and thought processes of scientists to improve science learning. As the implementation of these requirements in the classroom is a challenging goal, videos are effective tools for making aspects of scientific practices visible that often remain hidden for students. Current approaches in science education tend to adopt either an affective-volitional focus by authentic insights into scientific practices or a cognitive focus by promoting student skills in the context of SR or NOS. In this theoretical contribution, we argue for a synergistic integration of these foci. Following this argument, we derive a systematic framework for video development by combining insights from science education with the contextual analysis of science and technology studies and philosophy, which aims to open the black box of scientific practices while developing videos that systematically address students' skills and provide authentic insights into scientific research processes. In this paper, we present a new systematic framework for video development and provide examples of videos created using this framework for discussion within the scientific community.

articles.title: In the Mind or in the World? Types of Beliefs and the Locality of Evidence
articles.authors: Joseph Sommer
articles.abstract: People’s beliefs appear to be divisible into two distinct types. Some beliefs refer directly to the observable world, readily guide behavior, and are easily revised when challenged. On the other hand, ideological, political, and religious beliefs are associated with much more obscure evidence, often do not lead to behavior, and appear immune to counterevidence. To explain this intuitive difference between beliefs, several theories in psychology have proposed that the mind possesses two qualitatively different types of belief with different cognitive properties. Abelson (1986) distinguished testable from distal beliefs, where the former are updated on the evidence while the latter are held for instrumental reasons. Sperber (1997) proposed that intuitive beliefs are automatically formed by the mind, while reflective beliefs are only meta-represented, keeping them sequestered from other thoughts. Van Leeuwen (2023) divides beliefs into factual beliefs and credences, arguing that many ideological beliefs are a cognitive attitude more like imagination than true belief. In this paper, I draw on three neglected theories in psychology from the 1950s and suggest that they can explain apparent differences among beliefs without positing a novel type of belief. In so doing, I critique an assumption underlying types-of-beliefs theories: that an appeal to psychological properties is needed to explain why beliefs appear to differ from each other. In contrast, I suggest that differences among beliefs may be explained by a non-psychological characteristic of the world. This emphasis on studying the environment is inspired by Brunswik (1952, 1956). Relatedly, Bruner, Goodnow, and Austin (1956) suggested that concepts might differ not in the psychological processes used to classify them, but in the cues that contribute to their category-membership. Importing their notion of attribute immediacy to belief, I propose that beliefs differ in the locality of evidence that supports them. When most evidence is local, e.g., in beliefs based on perception, people’s beliefs often converge. However, in domains like politics, much of the relevant evidence is unavailable for scrutiny, allowing disagreements to persist. I develop a continuum of the proportion of evidential locality from deduction – which produces certain conclusions from fully local evidence – to perceptual and highly isotropic (Fodor, 1983) beliefs, which represent decreasing locality and certainty. An important consequence of non-local evidence is March and Simon’s (1958) uncertainty absorption, where conclusions are transmitted without their supporting evidence or reasoning. In domains with low evidential locality, we often need to defer to experts, with the result that we are unaware of the evidence supporting their conclusions. This may help beliefs in such non-local domains avoid counterevidence – beliefs cannot be conclusively refuted if their believers are unaware of their own reasons. I discuss how locality of evidence and uncertainty absorption provide a parsimonious account of a series of phenomena which motivated proposals of types-of-beliefs and suggest future directions for research into non-local evidence.

articles.title: The Impact of Emotions, Flow and Addiction on Time Perception in Short Videos: An Experimental and Survey Study
articles.authors: Yaqi Jiang; Zeyang Yang; Zaheer Hussain
articles.abstract: Short-form video platforms, including TikTok, have rapidly gained popularity. Their emotionally charged, algorithm-curated content can promote addiction and distort time perception. However, there is a scarcity of research on time perception within the realm of short videos. This study aimed to explore the influence of short video emotions, individual emotions, short video addiction, and flow on time perception using experimental and survey methods. Study 1 recruited 158 participants (Mean age = 21.50 years, SD = 2.16), and employed a 2 (time estimation paradigm: prospective, retrospective) × 3 (video emotion: positive, neutral, negative) between-subjects experimental design to investigate the impact of short video emotions, time estimation paradigms, short video addiction, and flow on time perception. Findings revealed that short video emotions influenced time perception, with positive videos leading to an underestimation of duration and a faster perception of time passage. Additionally, both short video emotions and addiction predicted the perception of time passage through flow. Study 2 conducted a survey of 525 participants (Mean age = 21.53 years, SD = 2.14) and utilised structural equation modelling to explore the associations between individual emotions (depression, anxiety, boredom), short video addiction, flow, and time perception. Depression and short video addiction predicted time perception through flow, further clarifying the relationships among these variables. The findings extend the I-PACE model and flow theory to popular short-video media, highlighting the role of flow in linking emotions, addiction, and time perception during short-video use.

articles.title: The Problem of Common False-Alarm Rates and the Informational Asymmetry When Comparing Pure and Mixed Lists in Recognition Memory:  Reconsidering the List-Strength Effect
articles.authors: Raz Danino; Yonatan Goshen-Gottstein
articles.abstract: The list-strength effect (LSE)—the interaction between item strength and list composition (pure versus mixed)—is theoretically diagnostic in models of memory. In contrast to free recall, in old-new recognition, the LSE was long considered negligible. This dissociation prompted the development of new recognition models. Recent work, however, suggests that the magnitude and direction of the LSE depend on the strength manipulation. Here, we argue that conclusions about the LSE are undermined by two methodological limitations in pure-mixed comparisons: a design flaw and an informational asymmetry. Sensitivity measures in recognition memory depend jointly on hit and false-alarm rates (HRs and FARs), which co-vary when sensitivity, response bias, or both change. When strength conditions differ only for studied items, sensitivity estimates from pure and mixed lists are not constructed from equivalent components. This is because traditional mixed-list designs, which impose a common FAR across strength conditions, are compared to pure lists wherein FARs can vary freely. Critically, there is no contingency in which given differences in HRs, a common FAR of any magnitude would lead researchers to attribute those differences to bias rather than to sensitivity—regardless of which sensitivity measure is used. We consider two possible arguments in favor of common FARs and refute both. Using a hypothetical reanalysis of recent LSE data, we show that empirically plausible changes in FAR—without altering observed HRs—can produce LSEs of any direction. We conclude that pure-mixed comparisons cannot adjudicate between theories of recognition memory. Alternative experimental designs are then discussed.

articles.title: The Impact of Emotions, Flow and Addiction on Time Perception in Short Videos: An Experimental and Survey Study
articles.authors: Yaqi Jiang; Zeyang Yang; Zaheer Hussain
articles.abstract: Short-form video platforms, including TikTok, have rapidly gained popularity. Their emotionally charged, algorithm-curated content can promote addiction and distort time perception. However, there is a scarcity of research on time perception within the realm of short videos. This study aimed to explore the influence of short video emotions, individual emotions, short video addiction, and flow on time perception using experimental and survey methods. Study 1 recruited 158 participants (Mean age = 21.50 years, SD = 2.16), and employed a 2 (time estimation paradigm: prospective, retrospective) × 3 (video emotion: positive, neutral, negative) between-subjects experimental design to investigate the impact of short video emotions, time estimation paradigms, short video addiction, and flow on time perception. Findings revealed that short video emotions influenced time perception, with positive videos leading to an underestimation of duration and a faster perception of time passage. Additionally, both short video emotions and addiction predicted the perception of time passage through flow. Study 2 conducted a survey of 525 participants (Mean age = 21.53 years, SD = 2.14) and utilised structural equation modelling to explore the associations between individual emotions (depression, anxiety, boredom), short video addiction, flow, and time perception. Depression and short video addiction predicted time perception through flow, further clarifying the relationships among these variables. The findings extend the I-PACE model and flow theory to popular short-video media, highlighting the role of flow in linking emotions, addiction, and time perception during short-video use.

articles.title: Three-Step State Space Mixture Modeling to Compare Dynamic Processes Across Many Individuals
articles.authors: Manuel T. Rein; Leonie V. D. E. Vogelsmeier; Jeroen Vermunt; Kim De Roover
articles.abstract: Researchers are often interested in modeling unobserved heterogeneity in dynamic processes of latent variables between individuals. This can be achieved with state space mixture modeling, a method that identifies discrete subgroups of individuals who follow qualitatively distinct processes. However, jointly estimating the measurement model of the latent variables, the structural model (the dynamic process), and the mixture components can be computationally demanding and fit indices conflate measurement model fit and structural model fit. Separating the estimation of measurement model and structural model offers faster computation time, easier interpretation of fit indices and various other advantages. To leverage these advantages, we present Three-Step State Space Mixture Modeling, an extension of the Three-Step Latent Vector Autoregression framework to state space mixture modeling. We demonstrate the method’s performance in obtaining correct estimates of the structural parameters and cluster memberships by means of a simulation study and illustrate the method with an empirical example.

articles.title: Takes one to open many: A field study assessing a representative contact Intervention
articles.authors: Nechumi Malovicki-Yaffe; Eric Shuman; Boaz Hameiri
articles.abstract: Intervening to improve intergroup relations is a complex and resource-intensive endeavor. Here, to promote better intergroup relations efficiently and effectively, we examine a school-based intergroup contact intervention, involving a group of (majority) group members and a representative of a minority group (i.e., representative contact). We assess this approach in a large-scale field study in 41 schools in Israel and the West Bank, in which Ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) representatives engaged in a 2-hour face-to-face discussion with groups of non-Haredi adolescents. Compared to pre-intervention levels, participants that engaged in representative contact exhibited less prejudice, more positive meta-perceptions, perceived the norms in their school to be more tolerant, and showed more willingness to learn about and engage in future meetings with Haredi group members. Our findings provide evidence from the field for the potential utility of representative contact in schools, as a cost-effective intervention to improve intergroup relations.

articles.title: Why faster thinkers are smarter thinkers: Working memory binding as the link between processing speed and cognitive ability
articles.authors: Anna-Lena Schubert; Linda Eileen Leydecker; Henrike Maria Jungeblut; Mareike Hülsemann
articles.abstract: Individuals who process information faster tend to score higher on cognitive ability tests, but the mechanism linking processing speed to general cognitive ability (g) remains unresolved. We tested whether this association is mediated by working memory capacity, and if so, whether the critical component is executive attention — as captured by complex span tasks requiring dual-task coordination and interference management — or relational binding — the ability to form and maintain temporary associations between unrelated features. In a community sample of 215 adults (aged 18–40), we measured processing speed at both the neural level (ERP component peak latencies) and the cognitive level (diffusion model drift rates), working memory through complex span and binding tasks, and g through a comprehensive cognitive ability test battery. Latent variable mediation analyses showed that complex span performance only partially mediated the speed–g relationship, leaving significant direct effects. In contrast, binding task performance fully mediated the relationship for cognitive processing speed and substantially mediated it for neural processing speed, reducing previously significant direct paths to nonsignificance. When both mediators competed in parallel models, binding emerged as the dominant predictor of g, with complex span contributing no unique variance. These findings identify relational binding as the specific working memory component that transmits the association between processing speed and cognitive ability, suggesting that the cognitive bottleneck linking processing speed to reasoning ability is not executive control but the more elemental ability to build and maintain temporary relational representations.

articles.title: Advancing therapeutic approaches for agitation: a neurorelational perspective
articles.authors: Kathy Liu; George Salaminios; Mara Mather; Rob Howard
articles.abstract: Agitation is a common, distressing and difficult-to-treat symptom in dementia, linked to emotional dysregulation and interpersonal stress. Degenerative and compensatory changes in the locus coeruleus-noradrenergic (LC-NA) system have been implicated in agitation in Alzheimer’s disease. However, neuropathology alone does not fully explain why some individuals are more prone to agitation than others. We explore how premorbid mentalizing capacity (the ability to understand one’s own and others’ mental states) in both care recipients and caregivers may influence agitation propensity. Mentalizing, rooted in early attachment experiences, supports emotion regulation but may be compromised under stress in the context of neurodegeneration and neuropsychiatric symptoms, and this could increase the likelihood of escalation of care. We propose a neurorelational framework for understanding and addressing agitation, integrating neurobiological and socio-emotional perspectives. Targeting both care recipients’ LC-NA system and caregivers’ mentalizing capacity may represent a personalized intervention strategy to mitigate effects of neurodegeneration on ‘neuropsychiatric reserve’.

articles.title: CATHA: A Therapist-Led Workflow for Linking Speech, Voice, and Clinical Meaning in Music Therapy
articles.authors: Sllobodan Gjerga
articles.abstract: CATHA (Clinical Audio Translation for Therapeutic Attunement) is introduced as a therapist-guided R/Shiny methodology designed to preserve the formal aspects of voiced expression in music therapy, with particular application in oncology and palliative care settings. The method is grounded in the philosophical concept of “speech seeking orientation under finitude,” which posits that speech in the context of serious illness not only conveys symptoms, preferences, or beliefs, but also seeks orientation, relational connection, containment, and a livable position within altered life circumstances. The accompanying philosophical manuscript contends that such speech is conveyed not only through semantic content but also through pacing, contour, hesitation, silence, recurrence, breath, and reaction, and that music therapy is uniquely positioned to address these expressive modalities. CATHA translates this insight into a transparent mixed-methods workflow comprising audio processing, waveform inspection, therapist-defined segmentation, acoustic feature extraction, score-like contour rendering, and uncertainty marking. The method is not intended as an emotion-recognition system, diagnostic tool, or predictor of clinical outcomes. Rather, CATHA serves as a methodological prototype for integrating transcript, vocal form, therapist interpretation, and reproducible research artefacts. This method's paper outlines the conceptual foundation, implementation logic, intended application in music therapy, validation strategy, and ethical considerations of CATHA. CATHA is available as an R/Shiny prototype (https://github.com/sgjerga/CATHA) and as an online application (https://sgjerga-catha.share.connect.posit.cloud/).

articles.title: From Wounded Healers to Commercialized Caregivers: A Scoping Review of Mental Health Content Creators on Social Media
articles.authors: Nicole Bizzotto; Qianfeng Lu; Srusti Bhalerao; Krysten Stein; Amanda Yarnell; Tobias Dienlin; Laura Marciano
articles.abstract: An increasing number of content creators, including clinicians and people with lived experiences, are sharing mental health–related content on social media. This review maps the empirical literature on mental health content creators, with the aim of synthesizing evidence on motivations for content creation and engagement, communicative practices, and associated outcomes for both creators and their audiences. In addition, it advances a working definition of mental health content creators that integrates and extends definitions identified in the literature. The search across nine databases identified 59 peer-reviewed studies published between 2018 and 2025, examining a wide range of platforms, most commonly YouTube (35.6%), TikTok (23.7%), and Instagram (20.3%). Most studies focused on content creators with lived experience of mental disorders (81.4%), whereas only a small minority examined those with professional expertise (8.5%). Beyond mental health conditions in general terms (32.2%), the most frequently shared topics were neurodevelopmental disorders (15.3%), feeding and eating disorders (13.6%), and depressive disorders (10.2%). Communicative practices were investigated across four domains: psychoeducation (addressed in 66.1% of studies), service promotion (5.1%), self-disclosure (47.5% reported positive framings; 47.5% reported negative framings), and advocacy (30.5%). The included studies reported a wide range of motivations for content creation, spanning from altruistic intentions to help others to self-validation. Motivations underlying audience engagement remained comparatively underexplored. Across studies, findings reveal persistent tensions between platformed care and commerce, highlighting how platform logics such as algorithmic visibility shape disclosure practices and audience engagement. Studies most frequently reported positive effects for audiences (47.5%), such as increases in mental health literacy. Fewer identified negative effects (13.6%), including phenomena such as mass social media–induced illness, while 30.5% identified mixed effects, reporting both beneficial and adverse impacts within the same study. For content creators, documented outcomes were also heterogeneous, and included not only a sense of community, validation, and monetization opportunities but also psychological costs such as risks of burnout.

articles.title: Affective Measures for Italian (AMI): Valence, Arousal and Dominance Norms for 13,495 Italian Nouns, Adjective and Verbs
articles.authors: Marco A. Petilli; Alessandra Vergallito; Marco Marelli; Simona Amenta
articles.abstract:
Words affective properties influence a broad range of cognitive processes and should be carefully considered when selecting verbal stimuli for experimental tasks. Although large-scale affective norm databases are available in several languages, Italian still lacks such an extensive resource. This limitation is relevant given the rich inflectional structure of Italian, which offers unique opportunities to investigate how morpho-syntactic variations may shape words affective processing.
To address this gap, we present a large-scale database collecting ratings of valence, arousal, and dominance for 13,495 Italian words, including three major parts of speech (PoS): nouns, adjectives, and verbs. Singular forms were included for nouns; masculine and feminine singular forms for adjectives; and, for verbs, infinitives together with first- and third-person singular present-tense forms of the indicative mood. 
Analyses showed that the distribution patterns of Italian affective ratings are largely consistent with those reported in previous affective norming studies. The relationship among the affective dimensions displayed the typical structure observed in the literature: valence and dominance showed a strong positive linear association, whereas arousal displayed significant quadratic U-shaped relationships with both valence and dominance, highlighting higher activation for words receiving extreme ratings in valence and dominance. These patterns were substantially replicated across grammatical classes.
The present dataset expands previous affective resources available for Italian and provides a reliable tool for psycholinguistic, cognitive, and cross-linguistic investigations. Crucially, the inclusion of multiple PoS and morphological forms makes this dataset particularly suitable for exploring how lexical and grammatical properties contribute to affective word processing.

articles.title: 404: Personal Data Not Found — Efficacy of Anonymous Internet-Based Self-Help for Social Anxiety in a Partially Randomized Patient Preference Trial
articles.authors: Stefanie Arnold; Michael Vogt; Dajana Šipka; Johanna Boettcher; Friederike Fenski; Malte Elson; Thomas Berger
articles.abstract:
Background:
Internet-based interventions can improve access to evidence-based treatments. However, concerns about privacy and data security remain barriers to their adoption. Designing interventions that allow anonymous use may reduce these barriers, but whether anonymity affects treatment efficacy remains unclear.
Objective:
This study compared clinical outcomes of an anonymous internet-based self-help intervention for social anxiety to a conventional non-anonymous version and investigated whether participants’ preferences regarding anonymity influenced treatment outcomes.
Methods:
In this partially randomized patient preference trial, 452 adults with clinically relevant social anxiety symptoms were assigned by randomization or preference to an anonymous or non-anonymous 8-week unguided cognitive behavioral self-help program. Assessments were conducted at baseline, post-treatment, and 24-week follow-up. The primary outcome was social anxiety severity. Mixed-effects models were used to evaluate changes over time and differences between program versions.
Results
Participants showed significant reductions in social anxiety symptoms over time with medium to large within-group effect sizes (d = -0.81 to -0.62). No significant superiority of the non-anonymous version was observed in the randomized or preference arms. Secondary outcomes including depressive symptoms, quality of life, and internalized stigma also improved. Effects were maintained at follow-up and did not differ by allocation method. Further exploratory analyses did not provide robust evidence of outcome differences by preference match.
Discussion
The anonymous intervention was not outperformed by the conventional non-anonymous program. These findings provide preliminary evidence that anonymity need not compromise intervention efficacy and strong privacy protections may help address privacy-related barriers.

articles.title: Serial Dependence Scales with Action-binding Depth in Duration Perception
articles.authors: JiaoWu; Aylin Gündogdu; Halit Can Akgün; Ceren Songur; Zhuanghua Shi
articles.abstract: Serial dependence biases perception toward recent history, yet it often appears only when observers respond actively. The critical issue, however, is whether the effect depends on motor output itself or on how strongly stimulus features are built into the response plan. We tested this by comparing active duration reproduction with passive following, where observers simply tracked stimulus onset and offset with a sustained keypress. Twenty-four participants completed both tasks with physically matched sensory input, equating stimulus properties and motor execution, while the degree of coupling between duration and action varied. The sequential bias was strongest for reproduction-after-reproduction trials, weaker for reproduction-after-following trials, and minimal when the current trial required following. Passive following still showed central tendency, with offset latency revealing implicit duration encoding, but it generated little attraction across trials. These findings point to action-binding depth, rather than motor output alone, as the key driver of serial dependence in time perception.

articles.title: Pseudo-Factor Analysis with Item Intercepts
articles.authors: Nigel Guenole
articles.abstract:
We extend the pseudo-factor analysis method by adding a mean
structure to obtain pseudo-intercepts.We do so by substituting the item
response mean vector based on human responses with a scalar vector
obtained by projecting item embeddings via dot products onto semantic
intensity axes capturing the difference between low and high scale
anchors. Across six scales measuring the trait domains of the DSM-5
alternative model for personality disorders in occupational settings,
the mean correlation between empirical and pseudo-intercepts was
r = .54, while the mean correlation between empirical and pseudointercepts
obtained via deliberate projection onto randomly incorrect
semantic axes was r = −.35. The inclusion of embedding-based
means proxies in pseudo-factor analysis can potentially enhance scale
development and allow mean and covariance structure modeling without
empirical data. This may open the possibility of examining relative
item locations, multiple-group pseudo-modeling, and measurement invariance
analyses. Topics such as systematic methods to identify semantic
anchors and risks including ‘anchor hacking’ are discussed.

articles.title: Feedback anticipation and integration in risk preferences from childhood to elderhood
articles.authors: Antonios Nasioulas; Oana Stanciu; Maël Lebreton; Stefano Palminteri; Azzurra Ruggeri
articles.abstract: Risk preferences are shaped by outcome anticipation and feedback integration. Understanding how these processes evolve across the human lifespan is essential for theoretical models of decision-making, for applications in education, and behavioral public policy aimed at protecting against age-related vulnerabilities. This study examines the lifespan development (N = 658; ages 8 to 78) of decision-making under risk through the lens of these two constitutive components, using a behavioral paradigm merging descriptive and experiential information. Our findings replicate the established pattern of a peak of risk-taking during adolescence, followed by a steady decline with aging. Moreover, our results show that while the influence of outcome anticipation matures gradually during childhood and adolescence, the propensity to learn from feedback diminishes progressively with age. These results suggest that while adults may rely on more sophisticated internal models of probability, these models may paradoxically support faulty reasoning, potentially contributing to suboptimal choices.

articles.title: Making Food VAT Reform Tangible: Concrete Price Information Increases Public Acceptance
articles.authors: Hellen Temme; Cornelia Betsch; Philipp Sprengholz
articles.abstract: Increasing the value-added tax (VAT) on animal- and reducing it on plant-based foods can reduce greenhouse gas emissions and diet-related disease burden by shifting consumption toward more sustainable dietary patterns. Yet, public acceptance remains low. Two pre-registered online experiments in Germany examined whether acceptance is linked to cost overestimation and whether communicating accurate price information increases public support. In Study 1 (N = 161), participants submitted a personal grocery receipt and were randomly assigned to one of three VAT scenarios: an increase on animal-based products (7% to 19%), a reduction on plant-based products (7% to 0%), or both. Policy acceptance was measured before and after participants estimated the price change of their receipt and received feedback on the actual change. Although participants systematically misestimated costs, initial acceptance (β = .92, p < .001) rather than misestimation (β = .03, p = .329) was the dominant predictor of subsequent acceptance. When given the opportunity to eliminate products from their receipt after receiving feedback on the actual change, participants predominantly removed meat and dairy. Study 2 (N = 299) focused on the VAT combination policy using a standardized €30 receipt. Participants either estimated costs before receiving feedback, received accurate price information only, or received only a policy description. Acceptance in the estimation (d = .54) and information (d = .33) conditions significantly increased compared to the control condition, while misestimation showed no significant relationship with acceptance. These findings suggest that expressing price changes in Euros rather than VAT percentages represents a simple, scalable strategy for increasing public support for sustainable food taxation.

articles.title: Memorability as Emergent Property: How Representational Structure Creates Apparent Intrinsic Effects
articles.authors: Dyllan Simpson; John Wixted; Timothy F. Brady
articles.abstract: Some pictures, videos, faces, voices, and words are reliably remembered better than others, a phenomenon known as memorability. This has been widely interpreted as reflecting a stable, intrinsic property of the stimuli themselves. Here we argue that this consistency is better explained by the relational structure of the stimulus sets used to measure memorability, rather than as an intrinsic property of the items. Using simulations with a global matching model of recognition memory, we show that apparent intrinsic memorability emerges naturally from distinctiveness — how dissimilar an item is from others in a given set of encoded images — without any stimulus-intrinsic mechanism. In behavioral experiments, we demonstrate that manipulating the similarity structure of image sets can invert memorability rankings, making previously memorable images forgettable and vice versa. We also show that features previously identified as intrinsically memorable (such as “body-part related”) are the most representationally distinctive features in standard databases, explaining their memorability. To address these large variations in distinctiveness we created a new image set that reduced variability in representational distinctiveness, and found it significantly diminishes all feature-memorability relationships. These findings suggest that memorability is not a fixed attribute of items themselves, but an emergent property of which items are distinctive within the context, with implications for how we attribute stable psychological properties to stimuli more broadly.

articles.title: Evaluating User Perspective on a Mindfulness Based- Intervention for Improving Risk and Protective Factors of Disordered Eating: A Person- Based Approach.
articles.authors: Vanshika Drolia
articles.abstract:
Objective: This study aimed to evaluate a brief  digital mindfulness intervention designed to target body image and eating disorder risk factors among young adults aged 16-34, as part of a wider mixed methods research taking a person-based approach during the development of a digital mindfulness-based intervention.
Method: A person-based approach was used involving think-aloud interviews and semi-structured interviews with 10 participants (5 Male, 5 Female) (mean age=26.5, SD=5.68)  without current eating disorders. Reflexive thematic analysis was conducted following Braun and Clarke's (2006) six-phase approach.
Results: Three main themes emerged - usability, acceptability, and feasibility of the tailored mindfulness program. Participants found the content clear, simple, coherent and engaging. They reported increased self-awareness and more adaptive thought patterns, indicating acceptability. All participants intended to continue practicing the techniques, reflecting feasibility. However, participants also mentioned practical challenges in maintaining focus during the mindfulness practices. The table of changes created after the first set of interviews allowed incorporation of modifications to address issues like reducing long pauses and adding more guidance during exercises based on initial user feedback.
Discussion: While an expanded trial is warranted, findings provide initial evidence that accessible, tailored digital mindfulness tools can successfully engage youth and cultivate protective skills relevant to body image and disordered eating prevention. Further research into optimized blended digital mindfulness interventions is needed as an engaging early prevention approach for escalating mental health risks among young people. The digital format enables scalable delivery on platforms youth already access.
Keywords: digital mindfulness-based intervention, person- based approach, disordered eating, young adults, prevention.

articles.title: The Cannabis Research Image Database (CRESIDA): a standardized and validated image set for studying cannabis cue-reactivity
articles.authors: Janna Cousijn; Emese Kroon; Chao Suo; Tom Freeman; Chandni Hindocha; Marianna Quinones-Valera; Valentina Lorenzetti
articles.abstract: Background and Aims: Cannabis cue-reactivity paradigms are instrumental in studying the behavioral and neurocognitive mechanisms of cannabis use and cannabis use disorders. However, image sets used for cannabis cue-reactivity paradigms vary between studies, and the lack of reliability and validity assessment hinders the quality of evidence they generate. The main aim of this study was to create a novel, open access, standardized, and representative database of cannabis use-related images including control images matched by resolution, luminosity, and complexity: The Cannabis Research Image Database (CRESIDA). The secondary aim was to examine whether subjective cannabis cue-induced craving was associated with cannabis‑use severity and whether this relationship was moderated by image type. As an illustrative example of how our open data can be used and how sample characteristics can shape cue‑reactivity, we also explored the role of cannabis–tobacco mixing by comparing cannabis cue-induced cannabis and tobacco craving between individuals who did and did not mix the substances. Design: An online survey was administered to participants recruited via online platforms, community advertisement, and snowballing. Setting: USA, the Netherlands, and Australia. Participants/Cases: 689 participants who consumed cannabis monthly-to-daily (385 men, 298 women, 6 other) were recruited between January 2022 and May 2024. Measurements: Out of 93 cannabis images and 93 matched neutral images, participants each rated 31 image pairs for cannabis craving (the primary outcome), arousal, valence, and tobacco craving. Participants were characterized for socio-demographic data, level of cannabis use and related problems, and mixing cannabis and tobacco. A subset of 78 images was selected for further analysis based on cannabis craving results. Image ratings were evaluated for internal consistency (). Furthermore, we examined the association between cannabis cravings and cannabis use characteristics, and explored if cannabis craving ratings were affected by image type (i.e., product, paraphernalia, and actions), and by using cannabis alone vs. mixing cannabis and tobacco.  Findings. The database showed excellent reliability ( = .995-.965). Cannabis craving, valence, and arousal discriminated cannabis and control images. More cannabis use days (β = 0.162, p < 0.001) and cannabis-related problems (β = 0.268, p < 0.001) were significantly associated with higher image-related cannabis craving. Mixing cannabis with tobacco, compared to using cannabis alone, was associated with the presence of tobacco craving in relation to cannabis images, and with greater cannabis craving in relation to cannabis images (β = -0.457, p < 0.001).  Conclusions: Images part of the open access CRESIDA (https://osf.io/dc9nz/) are reliable and valid for the scientific study of cue-reactivity internationally, providing a broad range of free to use cannabis and control images.

articles.title: Towards precision psychotherapy: Leveraging session-level data to predict session-by-session treatment outcomes
articles.authors: Felix Strakeljahn; Tania Marie Lincoln; Niklas Jacobs; Celine Brandt; Kevin Hilbert; Katrin Bahlinger; Inga Frantz; Björn Schlier
articles.abstract:
Theoretical Background: Clinical support tools can potentially enhance cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) by providing session-by-session treatment recommendations. To enable such tools, prediction models are needed that can accurately track therapy outcomes based on assessable session data. This study developed models that predict session-by-session mental health levels and identified variables that influence these predictions.
Methods: We analyzed data from a monocentric, naturalistic therapy-process study of outpatients with affective, anxiety, and psychotic disorders (n=327) participating in up to 24 weekly sessions of CBT in routine care in Germany. Within a nested cross-validation approach, we used mixed-effect random forest models to predict session-by-session scores on a brief self-report measure of mental health (i.e., symptoms, mood, distress, functioning) assessed in the next session. Included predictors were baseline psychopathology, type of CBT interventions conducted in the prior session, and patient- and therapist-reported mechanisms of change (e.g., cognitive change, self-understanding, coping) assessed in the prior session. Variable importance was examined using SHAP values.
Results: The mixed-effects random forest predicting next-session outcome achieved an R²=0.23 across the test folds. The most influential predictors were baseline psychopathology and prior session self-reported behavioral mechanisms of change, i.e., engagement in meaningful/positive activities and coping.
Conclusion: Session-by-session prediction of mental health outcomes using routine outcome monitoring data yielded promising predictive accuracy. Self-reported mechanisms of change are particularly valuable for predicting session-by-session mental health levels. These findings may suggest that activating behavioral mechanisms of change in therapy may have a stronger impact on short-term mental health than activating cognitive mechanisms of change.

articles.title: Within-person prospective associations between youth psychopathology and white matter development and the moderating role of parental mental health
articles.authors: Valerie Karl; Dani Beck; Lorenza Dall'Aglio; Niamh MacSweeney; Lucinda Sisk; Bailey Holt-Gosselin; Taylor Jay Keding; Lars T. Westlye; Tilmann von Soest; Dylan G. Gee
articles.abstract:
Background: Adolescence is a developmental period involving ongoing white matter (WM) maturation and rising risk for psychopathology. This risk is particularly elevated among youth whose parents have mental health problems. 
Methods: Leveraging longitudinal data from three waves of the community-based Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (N = 6,581, Nobservations = 16, 271, mean ageTP 1 = 10.2, mean ageTP 2 = 12.0 , mean ageTP 3 = 14.2), we employed random intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPMs) to disentangle stable between-person differences from time-varying within-person changes. We examined prospective and bidirectional associations between youth general psychopathology and diffusion metrics, including fractional anisotropy (FA) and mean diffusivity (MD) in the uncinate fasciculus (UF), superior longitudinal fasciculus (SLF), and corpus callosum (CC). We also tested whether parental psychopathology moderated these associations. 
Results: Preregistered analyses showed no significant between-person or within-person associations between parent-reported youth psychopathology and WM microstructure, nor evidence of moderation by parental psychopathology. In exploratory analyses using youth self-reports, SLF and CC FA were negatively associated with psychopathology at the between-person level, but positively associated at the within-person level during early adolescence. Additionally, SLF and CC MD were negatively associated with self-reported psychopathology on the within-person level during early adolescence, with this association persisting into mid-adolescence for the CC. Parental psychopathology moderated the within-person association between CC MD and self-reported youth psychopathology.
Conclusion: Overall, the findings did not support hypothesized prospective and bidirectional associations between parent-reported youth psychopathology and WM microstructure, nor moderation by parental psychopathology. Instead, our findings highlight limitations of tract-specific explanations for general psychopathology in at-risk youth, informant discrepancies, and the importance of separating between- and within-person effects.

articles.title: Orchestrating neuroimaging data processing using the 'Snakemake' workflow manager
articles.authors: Damien John Mannion; Maria del Mar Quiroga; Jacob M. Paul; Marta Garrido
articles.abstract: The processing of neuroimaging data typically involves a complicated set of operations, which often require different software packages and have intensive computational and storage demands. Although there are many options available for the neuroimaging researcher to establish their preferred set of processing operations, there is relatively little guidance on how to orchestrate such operations within a coherent data processing workflow. Here, we describe the workflow manager 'Snakemake' and introduce its conceptual foundations by referencing an example implementation of a simplified neuroimaging data processing workflow. Snakemake allows researchers to use a Python-based markup language to describe how input files are transformed into output files. Snakemake then uses these rules to schedule and execute the computational jobs that are required to generate the output files, without unnecessary recreation of existing output files. This allows the complete processing workflow to be executed with a single command and creates workflows that are interpretable, efficient, and reproducible.

articles.title: Bilingualism is Associated with Executive Function in Children With Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review and Bayesian Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Xuyi Wang; Jiayan Xie; Ruozhen Hu; Hongwei Ding; Yang Zhang
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Families and clinicians often express concern that bilingual exposure may place additional communicative or cognitive demands on children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD), potentially hindering development. In contrast, researchers generally report no negative effects, though it remains unclear whether bilingualism may confer cognitive benefits. We conducted a systematic review and Bayesian meta-analysis in order to evaluate the association between bilingualism and executive function (EF) in children with ASD.
Methods: We systematically searched five electronic databases and identified 11 studies comparing bilingual and monolingual children with ASD on EF measures (e.g., working memory, inhibitory control, cognitive flexibility). Across studies, 44 effect sizes and 809 participants were included. We implemented a multilevel Bayesian random-effects meta-analysis to estimate the overall association between bilingualism and EF.
Results: We found a small-to-moderate positive association between bilingualism and EF enhancement (Hedges’ g = 0.43, 95% CrI = [0.16, 0.71]). Importantly, the measured EF subdomain and EF measure type moderated this effect, whereas age and gender did not. Generalizability is limited by the predominance of Western, Indo-European samples (10/11 studies) and by heterogeneity across studies.
Conclusion: Contrary to the concerns about cognitive burden, we found limited pooled evidence for small-to-modest EF advantages, which varies across EF measures. Theoretically, our findings are consistent with accounts linking bilingual experience to EF processes. Clinically, maintaining bilingual environments for children with ASD can be beneficial with positive cognitive effects.
