Together, these findings show how individuals’ numerical abilities interact with the presentation of statistical information to shape vaccine-related judgments, highlighting both effective strategies for improving risk assessment and persistent challenges in communicating complex statistical principles.

articles.title: Standardizing Predictors for Computing Effect Sizes in Multilevel Modeling with Random Slopes
articles.authors: Guanyu Chen; Amery D. Wu; Yan Liu
articles.abstract: Reporting effect sizes has become standard practice in the social and behavioral sciences, and multilevel modeling is widely used for analyzing hierarchically structured data. However, calculating effect sizes for multilevel modeling with random slopes remains challenging because the variance of the random effects depends on the values of the predictor, resulting in heteroscedasticity. Existing solutions address this issue by treating the random effects variance as a mixture of individual variances, which can be computationally complex and difficult to interpret. This study addresses this gap by proposing a simple and practical alternative: standardizing predictors associated with random slopes to yield a straightforward variance decomposition that facilitates effect size calculation. We evaluate this approach using both an empirical data example and a simulation study. Results show that the predictor standardization approach is easy to implement and produces effect size estimates comparable to those obtained using the mixture distribution approach. This work provides a practical solution for effect size reporting in multilevel modeling with random slopes and supports broader adoption of effect size measures in applied multilevel research.

articles.title: Daily symptom associations in prolonged grief: a dynamic network approach
articles.authors: Huy Hoang Le; Kai Jannik Nehler; Mirjam Sophie Rueger; Qiqi Liang; Ilena Fruchi; Eva-Maria Stelzer; Clare Killikelly
articles.abstract:
Background: Prolonged grief disorder (PGD) is characterized by fluctuating, wave-like reactions that unfold over hours, yet little is known about the short-term temporal dynamics among daily grief symptoms. 
Objective: This study examined within-person temporal and contemporaneous symptom networks using intensive longitudinal data. 
Method: Thirty bereaved adults who lost a loved one on average 19.5 months ago completed a daily sampling protocol for 2 weeks (5 prompts/day; 3-hour intervals). The assessment adapted 14 items from the International Prolonged Grief Disorder Scale (IPGDS) (e.g., “In the past three hours, I have felt a longing or yearning for the deceased”). Multilevel vector autoregressive models estimated a lagged temporal network and a contemporaneous network (within-time partial correlations). 
Results: In the temporal network, difficulties accepting the loss showed the highest out-strength and predicted subsequent avoidance, difficulties engaging in social activities, and guilt. Preoccupation predicted later yearning. Emotional numbness and difficulties feeling joy predicted each other at subsequent time points, suggesting a short-term feedback process. Highest in-strength was observed for difficulties engaging in social activities, avoidance, and yearning. In the contemporaneous network, symptoms were densely interconnected; identity disruption, yearning, and sadness were most central. The strongest within-time association linked emotional numbness with difficulties feeling joy, and social-activity difficulties co-occurred strongly with guilt and blame. Acceptance-related unrealness emerged as a key short-term predictor of downstream avoidance and withdrawal, while a numbing–anhedonia loop may sustain affective constriction throughout the day. 
Conclusion: This study contributes to the understanding of the daily lives of bereaved individuals, highlighting grief as an interactive, mutually reinforcing dynamic system over time. Findings then suggest candidate mechanisms for exposure and behavioral activation interventions but should be considered hypothesis-generating for larger replications.

articles.title: Empirically derived phenotypes can reduce diagnostic heterogeneity
articles.authors: Miriam K. Forbes; John F. Buss; Daniel P. Moriarity; Eiko I Fried; Holly Frances Levin-Aspenson; Lorenzo Lorenzo-Luaces; Kasey Stanton; Ashley L. Watts; Gregory W. Forbes
articles.abstract: Diagnostic heterogeneity is a longstanding challenge for the classification of mental illness because people who receive the same diagnosis can differ markedly in their symptom presentations as well as in the mechanisms underlying those symptoms. High heterogeneity undermines both the clinical utility of diagnostic labels and the scientific accuracy of research that treats disorders as coherent constructs. In this study, we examined heterogeneity across six internalizing disorders (specific phobia, social anxiety disorder, agoraphobia, generalized anxiety disorder [GAD], major depressive disorder [MDD], and posttraumatic stress disorder [PTSD]) using three complementary approaches to quantifying heterogeneity: 1) descriptive heterogeneity (how many symptom profiles can theoretically satisfy the diagnostic criteria, and how many are observed in practice), 2) person-focused heterogeneity (whether diagnostic labels capture people with similar symptom presentations), and 3) construct-focused heterogeneity (whether diagnostic labels group symptoms with similar patterns of association with external validators). Analyses drew on subsamples from a large convenience sample with elevated psychopathology, predominantly from Australia and the USA (n = 14,762, mean [SD] age = 38.0 [18.0]; 55% women). Results showed that specific phobia was consistently the least heterogeneous; social anxiety disorder, agoraphobia, and GAD had mixed results; and MDD and PTSD were highly heterogeneous across all metrics. Notably, empirically derived phenotypes nested within each disorder substantively reduced heterogeneity relative to the DSM diagnoses. The study provides both a systematic multi-method approach to characterising different types of diagnostic heterogeneity, and a practical path toward more precise and informative constructs that can be implemented by rescoring existing data.

articles.title: Shape features of white matter tracts associated with post-surgical speech production outcomes
articles.authors: Greig Ian de Zubicaray; Katherine Drummond; Emma Murton; Benjamin Ong; Rosalind Jeffree; Valeriya Tolkacheva; Katie McMahon
articles.abstract: Impairments in speech production occur at a high rate after surgical resection of primary brain tumours. Due to their spatial heterogeneity, tumours can be problematic for voxel-wise lesion-symptom mapping. In addition, lesion maps of resection cavities may not capture displaced/deformed tracts, limiting the accuracy of white matter disconnection approaches based on population average atlas templates. Here we explore whether shape features extracted from individualised diffusion MRI tractography such as volume, curvature, surface area, irregularity, and span of six language-related white matter tracts are able to predict long-term speech production outcomes in survivors of left hemisphere primary brain tumours: The arcuate (AF), uncinate (UF), inferior longitudinal (ILF), inferior frontal occipital (IFOF), superior longitudinal (SLF) fasciculi, and frontal aslant tract (FAT). Best fit regression models selected via cross-validation and both univariate and multivariate lesion-symptom mapping analyses including clinical and demographic variables were used to compare the predictive capability of tract shape features, atlas disconnection estimates, and lesion location. Lesion-symptom mapping failed to reveal any significant relationships with speech production outcomes. A model comprising atlas-based disconnection estimates from the UF, SLF, and FAT was able to significantly predict long-term speech production outcomes (R2 = 0.14). However, a model comprising shape features of the FAT, ventral (III) component of the SLF, and IFOF explained considerably more variance (R2 = 0.37). Finally, we show that, in this dataset, shape features uniquely predict 26% variance that is not accounted for by disconnection estimates, demonstrating their relevance for predicting speech production outcomes in post-surgical patients.

articles.title: Metacontingency Analysis of Practices Associated to Mental Health
articles.authors: Blanca Patricia Ballesteros de Valderrama; Monica Novoa Gomez
articles.abstract:
This article proposes an analysis of mental health and psychotherapy as a result of cultural practices, based on contextual behavioral science, which combines behavioral analysis and cultural analysis. Similarly, it proposes complementing the analysis of public policy and other regulatory instruments with analytical tools from this scientific perspective, such as metacontingencies and macrocontingencies, which allow us to understand a policy or any regulatory instrument as a metacontingency system capable of constructing cultural practices related to mental health and facilitate the analysis of molar and molecular factors in psychotherapy. Reference is made to the case of Colombia as a country with particular conditions that could be addressed with an integrated model. Finally, methodological challenges and implications are addressed.
Keywords: metacontingencies; macrocontingencies; cultural practices; psychotherapy; public policy and mental health.

articles.title: Attitudes Toward Open Science Practices Among German Psychologists
articles.authors: Veit Kubik; Isabel Thielmann; Carina G. Giesen; Kenneth Koslowski; Gordon Feld; Anna-Lena Schubert
articles.abstract: The wide adoption of Open Science (OS) practices is influencing psychological science positively by increasing openness, transparency, reproducibility, and replicability. Researchers are encouraged to openly share their data, materials, and reproducible code, and to pre-register their hypotheses, design, sample size, and analysis plans whenever possible. On the one hand, these OS practices incur objective and/or perceived benefits, thereby raising hopes among researchers. On the other hand, these practices may also incur objective and/or perceived costs, thereby potentially inducing worries, especially among early career researchers. To investigate researchers’ attitudes toward the application of OS practices in their research and teaching, we conducted a survey among members of the German Psychological Society (N = 596) in 2021. Across status groups, researchers agreed that psychological science generally profits from the application of OS practices and that OS practices will become commonplace in the future. Furthermore, researchers indicated increased trust in studies adopting OS practices. However, researchers of all status groups reported to mainly have experience with pre-registration but less or even no experience with other OS practices. Researchers, especially those early in their career, believed to profit from OS, but also reported worries of making errors, especially when it comes to openly sharing analysis scripts. We discuss the current state of OS implementation and propose interventions to facilitate the broader adoption of OS practices, thereby supporting the career progression of early career researchers.

articles.title: Before Flow: Pre-Task Motivation and Challenge Expectancy as Primary Antecedents
articles.authors: Oliver Durcan; Ozan vardal; Peter Holland
articles.abstract: Flow is the co-occurring experience of absorption, effortless control and intrinsic reward. Research on its antecedents has focused primarily on what unfolds during a task. Here, we tested how pre-task and within-task states jointly predict flow dimensions and its global composite, focusing on intrinsic motivation and task challenge. Forty-five pianists (134 observations) completed three tasks at their calibrated optimal challenge level: piano sight-reading, piano improvisation, and Tetris. When the four motivation types of self-determination theory were modelled simultaneously, only pre-task intrinsic motivation independently predicted flow (β = .34 to .72); identified regulation, external regulation, and amotivation contributed no unique variance. Intrinsic motivation alone also fit flow better than the broader autonomous-motivation composite (ΔAIC = 4.29 to 27.36). A further model added expertise, task architecture, and challenge expectancy deviation (the signed difference between calibrated optimum and experienced challenge) alongside intrinsic motivation. Pre-task intrinsic motivation, contributing at both person and task levels, was the only antecedent to predict global flow and accounted for most of its explained variance. Distinct antecedent profiles emerged across flow dimensions that the global score obscures. Intrinsic motivation was the strongest predictor of intrinsic reward (β = .67). Effortless control was instead best explained by challenge expectancy deviation, being highest when the task was less challenging than expected (β = -.27). Absorption was dominated by person-level intrinsic motivation alongside a task contrast. Flow appears contingent on the priors people bring into a task, not only on what unfolds during it.

articles.title: A Beta Way: A Tutorial on Bayesian Beta Regression for Psychological Research
articles.authors: Jason Geller; Robert Kubinec; Chelsea Parlett; Matti Vuorre
articles.abstract: Rates, percentages, and proportions are common outcomes in psychology and the social sciences. These outcomes are often analyzed using models that assume normality, but this practice overlooks important features of the data, such as their natural bounds at 0 and 1. As a result, estimates can become distorted. In contrast, treating such outcomes as Beta-distributed respects these limits and can yield more accurate estimates. Despite these advantages, the use of Beta models in applied research remains limited. Our goal is to provide researchers with practical guidance for adopting Beta regression models, illustrated with an example drawn from the psychological literature. We begin by introducing the Beta distribution and Beta regression, emphasizing key components and assumptions. Next, using data from a learning and memory study, we demonstrate how to fit a Beta regression model in R with the Bayesian package `brms` and how to interpret results on the response scale. We also discuss model extensions, including zero-inflated, zero- and one-inflated, and ordered Beta models. Basic familarity with regression modeling and R is assumed. To promote wider adoption of these methods, we provide detailed code and materials at https://github.com/jgeller112/Beta_regression_tutorial.

articles.title: Helpful but Not Trusted: Artificial Intelligence Adoption and Ethical Concerns Among Health Service Psychologists
articles.authors: Adam Lockwood; Ryan L. Farmer; Jessica McClintock
articles.abstract:
Artificial intelligence (AI) tools are increasingly available to psychologists, yet little is known
about how licensed health service psychologists are integrating these technologies into practice
or how they perceive their ethical implications. This descriptive survey examined AI use, ethical
perceptions, privacy and disclosure practices, barriers to adoption, and training needs among 518
members of the National Register of Health Service Psychologists. Roughly 57% reported using
AI for work purposes in the past six months; among AI users, 51.4% of AI users reported weekly
or daily use. The most common professional applications were answering work-related
questions, generating easy-to-understand explanations, creating presentation content, and writing
session or progress notes. Roughly 55% felt that AI is helpful in practice, whereas only 20.0%
agreed that they trust AI. Ethical concerns were application-specific, with the greatest concerns
involving AI-assisted test interpretation and report writing. Privacy and disclosure findings
suggested gaps between professional expectations and practice; 73.0% believed practitioners
should routinely disclose AI use to clients, yet reported disclosure was infrequent. Most AI users
reported that they had not entered protected health information into AI tools, although relatively
few reported using HIPAA-compliant vendors or business associate agreements. Among nonusers,
the most common barriers were ethical, privacy/confidentiality, and accuracy/reliability
concerns. Findings suggest that AI use in psychological practice is no longer hypothetical and
underscore the need for application-specific ethical guidance, privacy-compliance training, and
evidence-informed professional development.
Keywords: artificial intelligence, psychological practice, ethics, technology adoption,
health service psychologists
Introduction
The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in Professional Practice
The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies into professional practice has
accelerated dramatically in recent years. Reports from major consulting firms indicate that AI
adoption is reshaping how professionals across sectors—from healthcare to education to
business—accomplish their work (Singla et al., 2025; Microsoft & LinkedIn, 2024). These
technologies, particularly large language models capable of generating human-like text, have
made powerful AI tools accessible to professionals without specialized technical expertise. Tools
such as ChatGPT, Claude, and similar platforms have achieved unprecedented penetration into
the professional workforce, raising critical questions about how different professions are
adopting, adapting to, and ethically integrating these technologies into their practice (National
Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, 2024).

articles.title: Facial and body actions speak louder than context when inferring facial expressions of emotions in naturalistic images
articles.authors: Ruoying Zheng; Kevin Liu; Jiabin Zou; Chujun Lin
articles.abstract: Inferring others’ emotions is crucial for survival. Much work focused on facial expressions; others argue that rich body and contextual cues also shape emotion inferences from faces, often based on experimentally combined face images and contextual cues. However, people may use a different strategy to infer emotions in real-world contexts where rich cues naturally co-occur. Here, we had US representative participants (N = 475) view everyday images (N = 1,125) and infer the targets’ facial expressions of 8 emotions. We modeled how rich cues in the images quantified by computational algorithms and US representative participants (N = 1,093) predicted inferences of facial expressions of emotions. We found significant predictions by not only facial cues (e.g., action units, facial structure) but also body cues (e.g., posture, high-level actions) and contexts (e.g., environmental valence, gist). However, these predictions were based on different mechanisms: Facial and body actions carried unique information that was unexplainable by other co-occurring cues, whereas other cues primarily carried information that was shared. Given the differential mechanisms, we investigated whether integrating merely cues that carried unique information was sufficient for predicting human emotion inferences. We built artificial neural networks that allowed for process-level manipulations: by lesioning artificial neurons, we found that neurons that were crucial for human-like inferences integrated rich cues asymmetrically beyond facial and body actions. Together, these findings suggest that naturalistic inferences of facial expressions of emotions may be driven by facial and body actions that are contextualized by other co-occurring cues to support a coherent interpretation.

articles.title: Assimilation to External Cues: Comparing the Reliability of Anchoring Effects, Advice Taking, and Hindsight Bias and Exploring its Determinants
articles.authors: Tobias R. Rebholz; Julia Groß; Lukas Röseler
articles.abstract: People’s quantitative estimates, such as judgments about the height of a tower or the distance between two cities, can be influenced by external values, including random numbers in the environment or another person’s estimate. We reviewed three distinct but related phenomena in quantitative estimation: anchoring effects, advice taking, and hindsight bias. These effects are assimilative; that is, estimates are drawn toward the external value. They are also robust and they exhibit medium to large effect sizes. However, previous studies suggest that the reliability of their measurements differs considerably, ranging from low (anchoring) to high (advice taking). In this project, we compared the reliabilities of the three phenomena using open data (Study 1) and a new experiment employing a homogenized paradigm (Study 2). Homogenization involves controlling for differences in the informativeness of external values, computing assimilation scores, and using the same setting and knowledge domain. In Study 1, we found that, consistent with prior research, anchoring effects were largely unreliable, advice taking was reliably measured, and hindsight bias showed moderate reliability. This overall pattern was [reproduced] in [the simulation of] Study 2. We conclude that although methodological factors contribute to variability, they alone cannot explain differences in reliability.

articles.title: Salient Distractors Shift Both Perceptual Sensitivity and Response Criterion in Visual Search: A Meta-Analytic Reinterpretation of the Early Quitting Effect: Commentary on Moher (2020)
articles.authors: Yueqing Hu
articles.abstract: Moher (2020) attributed the early quitting effect (EQE)—faster target-absent responses and elevated miss rates in the presence of salient distractors—to a lowered quitting threshold. Yet this interpretation is underdetermined because the behavioral signatures are jointly consistent with a decline in perceptual sensitivity (d′), a conservative shift in response criterion (c), or both. We conducted a random-effects meta-analysis of signal detection theory (SDT) parameters across 13 independent samples from five published EQE studies. Both components were statistically reliable (Hedges' g = −0.36 for d′; Hedges' g = +0.36 for c), but heterogeneity analyses revealed that the criterion shift is more consistent across studies (I² = 38%) than the sensitivity decline (I² = 59%). We propose that salient distractors serve as heuristic anchors that reliably shift c, with secondary and context-dependent effects on d′. Adopting SDT decomposition as standard analytic practice—alongside network-resolved neuroimaging—would substantially advance mechanistic understanding of this practically important phenomenon.

articles.title: Gender as a Moving Target: Making Up “Woman” and How the Looping Effect Can Help Advance Psychological Theories
articles.authors: Luiza Yuan
articles.abstract: This paper argues that Ian Hacking’s concept of the looping effect refers to an empirical phenomenon, the analysis of which offers valuable insights for advancing psychological theorizing. As psychological knowledge increasingly permeates public discourse, accounting for looping effects in psychological theory and practice is more pertinent than ever. Psychological research often treats concepts like gender as stable, monolithic variables, despite evidence that psychological theories actively influence experiences, feelings, and behaviors. Current theorizing on gender fails to capture the complex ways women interact with both traditional and feminist concepts of womanhood, particularly in highly psychologized societies. Analyses of psychologization’s influence on the looping effects of “woman” are illustrated through, and informed by, excerpts from interviews with women from 12 countries. This paper extends Hacking’s account by proposing that looping effects are dynamic, context-dependent phenomena affected by variables such as psychologization, and should be empirically studied to improve theorizing and interventions. The interviews suggest that updating psychological theories of gender, including those applied to affective disorders and workplace inequalities, to reflect the growing influence of feminist ideals and psychologization on women’s experiences, feelings, and behaviors may better achieve scientific aims of explanation, prediction, and intervention in the West. Theoretical frameworks and methodological recommendations for capturing the looping effect to improve psychological theorizing are presented. I show how feminist conceptions of scientific objectivity ground these proposed frameworks and recommendations. (226 words)

articles.title: Vitamin D intake during pregnancy is associated with attenuated Season-of-Birth effects on infants’ language.
articles.authors: Luis Eduardo Muñoz; Hannah Fatima Kvernberg Dajani; Beatrice Cadier Pedersen; Eivor Fredriksen; Natalia Kartushina; Roger Mundry; Vibeke Moe; Lars Smith; Marian Kjellevold; Maria Wik Markhus
articles.abstract:
The time of birth within the year can influence life outcomes, a phenomenon known as a "season-of-birth effect". While this effect is well documented in relation to health, its impact on early development is poorly understood. We investigated this effect in the context of
infant language and cognitive development in Norway. In a first cross-sectional study, using parental reports of vocabulary development with 1187 infants, we found that 12-month-olds born in mid-April had lower expressive vocabulary than those born in mid-October. The second longitudinal study assessed 848 infants between 6 and 18 months of age using the Bayley Scales of Development and showed that infants born in mid-April had lower cognitive scores than those born in mid-October. Although both studies indicated lower scores for infants born in late winter, the second study also showed that regular prenatal vitamin D intake mitigated these effects, reducing season-of-birth effects for language development. These findings underscore the importance of adequate maternal vitamin D status to reduce seasonal disparities in early language development.

articles.title: Large language models are still far from replacing human participants
articles.authors: Yueqing Hu; Xinyang Peng; Wang Tianhong
articles.abstract: Despite impressive capabilities in simulating human behavior, large language models exhibit fundamental limitations that preclude their substitution for human participants in psychological research. A strategic reorientation toward complementary rather than substitutional approaches is therefore warranted.

articles.title: Assessing Deviations from Chance in a Spatial Guessing Task: Analytical Framework and Results from a Single Participant
articles.authors: Jim E. Lenz; Edward F Kelly; W. J. Ross Dunseath; David Acunzo
articles.abstract:
Experimental parapsychology research has long examined whether individuals can identify hidden
targets without conventional sensory cues, typically using forced-choice or free-response
paradigms. This paper presents a computerized task in which participants attempt to guess the
location of a hidden target on a screen, along with an analytical framework that evaluates whether
performance exceeds chance. After describing the statistical approach, which uses a continuous
measure of trial-wise response accuracy, we present data from a single participant whose
performance was previously described as above chance in similar studies. Overall, performance
did not exceed chance; however, a trend toward higher accuracy, though not surviving correction
for multiple comparisons, was observed in one condition. Subsequent analyses using Monte Carlo
simulations indicated that subtle differences in the spatial distribution of the random targets partly
accounted for this effect. We discuss how the present framework differs from conventional forced-
choice guessing tasks and emphasize the importance of post hoc analyses for drawing valid
statistical inferences.

articles.title: Testing within-person correlates of daily fearlessness about death in young adults
articles.authors: Emilia Sherifi; Avery Dixon; Ashley Filion; Jeremy Gordon Stewart
articles.abstract: Fearlessness about death (FAD) reflects a reduced fear of dying that enables individuals to act on suicidal thoughts. Understanding how FAD changes in real time is critical because suicide risk can escalate within hours. However, little is known about processes that influence FAD within brief time periods. Painful and provocative events (PPEs), affective lability and impulsivity have each been linked to suicidal behavior and may shape momentary increases in FAD, but prior studies have been largely cross-sectional. To address this gap, we enrolled 148 young adults with past year suicidal thoughts and/or behaviors to complete ecological momentary assessments five times per day for 21 days. Using multi-level modeling, we tested the impact of daily fluctuations in affective states (happy, sad, anxious, angry, ashamed, and hopeless) and impulsivity on same-day FAD, with daily engagement in PPEs as a mediator. Results indicated that greater daily lability in impulsivity, anger, sadness and shame was associated with increased same-day FAD. Contrary to hypotheses, these effects were not mediated by PPE engagement. Short-term increases in FAD may arise from rapid shifts in emotions and impulsivity, rather than experiences such as PPEs. Capturing these dynamic, within-person changes can refine suicide theory and improve real-time suicide risk assessment.

articles.title: Effects of active learning and talker variability on preschoolers' novel word learning
articles.authors: Federica Bulgarelli; Marina Rabideau
articles.abstract: Words sound different every time they are said, both by the same person and across people. This variability has been found to have mixed effects on learning. For example, 14-month-olds benefit from talker variability when learning minimal pairs, but struggle when learning distinct-sounding words. Talker variability is found to be particularly beneficial for learning to produce novel words. All of these previous studies have used passive exposure, presenting participants with predetermined orders or fixed types of variability. Here, we used an active learning paradigm wherein preschoolers could choose who to hear words from during word learning. Then, we tested whether participants seek out variability during word learning, how these talker variability choices influence word learning and whether actively making these choices results in different outcomes than passively receiving them. We find that preschoolers choose to hear words from multiple talkers, and talker variability choices interact with active learning to predict production, but not comprehension. Taken together, our results suggest that preschool aged children can make choices are benefit their own learning, informing theories of both talker variability and active learning.

articles.title: The Cost of Belonging—and Leaving:  Structure, Predictors, and Mental Health Outcomes of Costly Religious Exits
articles.authors: David Bridger Lisonbee; Yehudis Keller; Julie J. Exline; Daryl R. Van Tongeren; Sam Hardy
articles.abstract: Leaving a high-demand religion is often psychologically costly, yet most research focuses on simple outcomes rather than the structure and mechanisms of costly exit experiences. This study examined mental health outcomes associated with high-cost religious exits. Participants (N = 1221) had left the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS; n = 426), ultra-Orthodox Judaism (n = 292), fundamentalist Evangelical Christian (n = 279), and moderate/liberal Christian traditions (n = 209). Using a dimensional-categorical spectrum approach, results supported a single dimension of costly experiences composed of summed costs of leaving, negative exit experiences, spiritual harms and abuses, gender discrimination, religious/spiritual struggles, and demonic spiritual struggles. Higher costliness was predicted by religion of origin, gender, and sexual orientation. More intense costly experiences were associated with both higher PTSD symptoms and greater posttraumatic growth (PTG). Religious background moderated both outcomes: costly experiences predicted the highest PTSD among ultra-Orthodox Jewish individuals and was also elevated among LDS and fundamentalist evangelical participants, while ultra-Orthodox Jewish and fundamentalist evangelical participants reported less growth from their costly experiences. Gender moderated the relationship between costly experiences and PTG but not PTSD, with women experiencing less growth from their costly experiences. Sexual minority status moderated the relationship between costly experiences and PTSD, but not PTG. Overall, results suggest that the costly experiences associated with deidentification from high-demand religions begin not at exit, but during membership, and are strongly linked to both PTSD symptoms and PTG — with demographic moderators.

articles.title: Why Some Gestures Work Better Than Others: Toward a Model of Gesture-Supported Vocabulary Learning
articles.authors: Manuela Macedonia; Lihui Hu; Brian Mathias
articles.abstract:
Gesture has emerged as a powerful multimodal tool in vocabulary acquisition, with converging behavioral and neuroscientific evidence for its facilitative role in second language (L2) vocabulary learning. Yet in both instructional practice and experimental research, gestures are often treated as interchangeable, despite systematic differences in their form and implementation that may substantially shape learning outcomes. We examine the sources of such variability and argue that gesture design should be treated as a theoretically meaningful and methodologically important factor in L2 vocabulary instruction and research. 
Drawing on embodied cognition, cognitive psychology, educational neuroscience, and gesture studies, we synthesize seven mechanisms through which gestures may differentially support L2 vocabulary learning: iconicity, sensorimotor imagery, motional salience, emotional salience, gesture complexity fit, cultural familiarity, and enactment potential. We contend that variation along these dimensions may influence the multimodal lexical representations, their accessibility, and durability. 
Building on this framework, we introduce the Gesture Memory Support Model (GMSM), a preliminary model for rating gestures according to their predicted mnemonic support. The GMSM provides a principled basis for gesture quantification, enabling researchers to incorporate gesture-related variability more systematically into experimental design and statistical modelling, while helping educators to make more informed pedagogical choices.  By foregrounding gesture design as a critical determinant of learning outcomes, the GMSM aims to promote greater methodological rigor and theoretical precision in research on gesture supported vocabulary learning.

articles.title: Rethinking Talent in Vocabulary Learning:  A Developmental Perspective
articles.authors: Manuela Macedonia; Brian Mathias
articles.abstract: Educational systems have traditionally conceptualized talent in foreign language (L2) learning as a stable attribute that can be identified early through performance-based assessments. Within the domain of vocabulary learning (VL), this assumption has reinforced the view that some learners possess an inherent aptitude for acquiring new words, while others do not. This perspective article challenges such trait-based interpretations and argues instead for a developmental account of VL capacity. Here, VL is understood not as the passive expression of innate ability, but as a dynamic developmental process shaped by interacting cognitive, linguistic, affective, and contextual influences. We synthesize evidence showing that key components underlying successful VL in childhood—phonological and working memory, semantic network organization, fast mapping, metalinguistic and cross-linguistic awareness, lexical inference, and motivation—are malleable and responsive to instruction. We also highlight the role of socioeconomic context in shaping early L2 exposure and vocabulary trajectories, arguing that differences in outcomes are often misinterpreted as evidence of fixed talent rather than unequal opportunity. Neurocognitive findings demonstrate that the brain systems supporting word learning remain highly plastic across development and are shaped by learning conditions. Multisensory and embodied instructional approaches, in particular, have been shown to reduce performance gaps and disproportionately benefit lower-performing learners. On this basis, the article calls for a shift in VL educational practice and policy away from early selection and toward broad talent development. Educational systems should focus on designing learning environments that convert potential into achievement, thereby advancing both excellence and equity in language education.

articles.title: Influence of partial speaker face occlusion during audiovisual speech perception in age-related hearing loss
articles.authors: Patricia V. Aguiar; Brandon T. Paul
articles.abstract:
Objectives: Older adults with hearing loss appear to benefit more from seeing a talker’s face during speech-in-noise perception compared to their peers without hearing loss, which is presumed to reflect a visual compensation mechanism such as cross-modal neuroplasticity that offsets hearing deficits. How these visual benefits arise is unknown. We hypothesized that availability of facial features modulates the visual speech benefit in older adults with hearing loss, which would be driven by differential deployment of visual attention to facial features.

Design: Sixty-five adults aged 63–80 with typical hearing up to moderate hearing loss were recruited from the local community. Participants viewed audiovisual word stimuli and verbally reported the perceived word. Speech audio was presented in speech-shaped noise set to the 50% speech reception threshold. The task was done in four visual conditions: full face visible, eyes and upper face occluded, mouth and lower face occluded, and full face occluded. Eye-tracking was used to quantify dwell time on eye and mouth regions in all conditions except when the full face was occluded.

Results: Word perception was significantly higher when the full face or mouth were visible, compared to when the full face or mouth region were masked. Participants dwelled on the mouth most when the full face was visible, and on the mouth when eyes were masked, and on the eyes when the mouth was masked. Greater levels of high-frequency hearing loss were associated with worse word perception overall, but contrary to hypotheses, this did not depend on the availability of facial features. Calculation of a visual enhancement metric also did not correlate with level of hearing loss. No association was found between hearing loss and dwell time on facial features.

Conclusions: Results do not support that greater hearing loss in older adults is associated with enhanced audiovisual perception or altered visual attention to facial features.

articles.title: Too Little, Too Much: Evidence for Quadratic Patterns in Emotion Regulation Strategy Use in Daily Life
articles.authors: Dominique Maciejewski; Yasemin Erbas; Tak Tsun (Edmund) Lo; Egon Dejonckheere; Merlijn Olthof; Andrea Bunge; Eeske van Roekel
articles.abstract: Emotion characteristics, like emotion intensity and perceived controllability, shape how individuals regulate their emotions. While the link between emotion characteristics and emotion regulation strategies seems clear for some strategies, findings are mixed for others. This study proposes that individuals are most likely to engage in emotion regulation at average levels of emotion intensity and controllability, and less likely at low or high levels (i.e., when emotions are either too weak to require regulation or too overwhelming to regulate). To test this hypothesis, we used data from a 60-day diary study in 79 adults (mean age = 22.29, SD = 6.08, 84% female), who every evening reported on emotion characteristics (emotion intensity and controllability) and strategy use (distraction, rumination, reappraisal, expression, suppression, relaxation). In addition to single strategies, we also examined emotion regulation strategy switching (i.e., the extent to which individuals varied their strategy composition). We replicate some linear findings, including that expression, relaxation, and rumination were used on days with more intense and uncontrollable emotions (for expression only). Importantly, we provide novel evidence for quadratic relations, where suppression and reappraisal were used on days where emotions were of average intensity and controllability, and less on days where emotions were less or more intense and controllable than usual. Moreover, individuals were more likely to switch to strategies they typically do not use on days where emotions were less or more controllable than usual. These findings highlight the value of moving beyond linear relations when studying emotion regulation dynamics in daily life.

articles.title: Object and Setting Identification in Natural Auditory Scenes
articles.authors: Margaret Ann McMullin; Nathan C. Higgins; Rohit Kumar; Mounya Elhilali; Joel S. Snyder
articles.abstract: We encounter situations each day that require our auditory system to quickly interpret our surroundings. Auditory scene perception involves complex processes that allow us to identify both the setting and the objects within a scene, which are essential for decision-making, and situational awareness. While there is substantial evidence for distinct cortical regions and pathways supporting visual scene and object recognition, far less is known about how the brain processes complex auditory scenes and objects. This study aimed to determine whether distinct mechanisms underlie auditory setting and object identification and whether these mechanisms interact to aid perception. Participants listened to 200 natural auditory scenes of varying durations (1, 2, and 4 sec) and identified the setting (e.g., café) as well as the objects (e.g., talking, espresso machine, music) within each scene. Overall, performance was highest on the object identification task, and there was a significant interaction between task and scene duration, with a greater benefit of longer durations for the object identification task. Different low- and mid-level acoustic features of the scenes predicted performance on the two tasks. These results suggest that the auditory system employs distinct (and potentially interactive) computations for setting and object identification, allowing for quick interpretation of complex, real-world auditory scenes. The interaction between task performance and scene duration further suggests object and setting identification may operate on different temporal scales; objects might require more time for accurate individuation, while settings may be recognized more efficiently through global properties of scenes, such as openness or naturalness.

articles.title: You’ve got THIS! Demonstratives as a multimodal pathway to early word learning
articles.authors: Yayun Zhang; Dota Tianai Dong; Satoshi Tsutsui; Carolina Rodriguez Chavarria; Asli Ozyurek; Caroline F Rowland; Chen Yu; Paula Rubio-Fernandez
articles.abstract: Function words like `I' or `some' are highly frequent in children's linguistic input but are not learned early, so we ask: do they play any role in early word learning? Here we test a novel hypothesis that demonstratives, a class of function words like THIS and THAT in English, generate word learning opportunities for young children because they provide multimodal cues to joint attention on labeled objects. Combining head-mounted eye-tracking with machine-learning modeling of naturalistic interactions between parent and child (19 months), we show that demonstratives provide augmented inputs that scaffold word learning. Our results suggest that these function words orchestrate attentional dynamics in multimodal social interaction, expanding long-standing learning theories and showing that children can learn words from the full richness of everyday experience.

articles.title: The Calm Before the Cancel Culture? Development of the “Pressure for a Perfect Conduct” scale
articles.authors: Tiago Rôxo Aguiar; Diniz Lopes; Thomas R. Brooks
articles.abstract: Social media platforms have facilitated various ways to connect, influence, and surveil each other. A novel related concept is the idea of Cancel Culture, which previous research posits might impact the behavior, emotions, and cognitions of the general public. The construct of Pressure for a Perfect Conduct (PPC) has been proposed as a conceptual representation of the pressure individuals may experience to conduct themselves perfectly under the threat of being canceled. However, there is currently a lack of quantitative research employing this construct, as well as no validated instruments to assess it. With this study, we aimed to build and validate the first instrument capable of accessing the construct of PPC. We conducted three separate data collections: a quantitative collection with which we performed Exploratory Factor Analysis (Study 1, N = 304); a qualitative collection where we discussed the existing items with experts in related fields (Study 2a, N = 3); and a second quantitative collection with which we performed Confirmatory Factor Analysis (Study 2b, N = 307). Additionally, we tested and validated a short version of our instrument. Our results indicate that the proposed instrument is reliable and valid, and can safely be used to assess the PPC model, but warrants further adjustments. The proposed instrument can help unlock future research on the topic by providing a validated measure, grounded on a novel framework, capable of broadly collecting quantitative insights into the impacts of Cancel Culture at an individual level.

articles.title: Developmental variation from rational choice
articles.authors: Samuel David Jones; Dara Tan; Paul Rauwolf
articles.abstract: Rational choice theory offers a normative account of the allocation of bounded cognitive resources under constraints. This work has focused largely on static solutions, leaving the developmental implications of rational choice relatively under-researched. Conversely, developmental science has in general overlooked the role of an individual's choices, actions, and habits in shaping rather than merely reflecting developmental variation, from delay to psychopathology to expertise. The aim of this paper is to characterise the developmental implications of 'rational inattention', a leading framework within rational choice theory, and in doing so develop a normative account of how adaptive behaviour reciprocally shapes the emergence of diversity in intelligent behaviour. The thesis we develop is that rational choice - behaviour that is adaptive not only to the structure of the environment but to the constraints of the agent - shapes neurodevelopmental variation beyond the neurogenetic factors that promote that behaviour. We begin by presenting static solutions to rationally inattentive choice under constraints, before turning to adaptivity and learning over time. We demonstrate that adaptive behaviour contributes unique variance to developmental outcomes and predicts inertia in changing environments. Finally, we distil the key features of rationally inattentive behaviour and corresponding empirical support from the developmental science literature. The strength of this approach is to demonstrate that many of the signatures of diversity in intelligent behaviour across the lifespan can in principle fall out of a minimal set of rational assumptions. This has implications for how we think about and respond to individual differences in human development.

articles.title: Wellbeing, Energy, and Work Functioning Among Autistic and ADHD Employees: A Mixed-Methods Longitudinal Study of the Hybrid Digital Support Tool Brain in Hand
articles.authors: Matthew Scott; Abi Jones; Jess Titley; Helen Guyatt
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Autistic and ADHD people experience persistent employment inequalities. This mixed-methods longitudinal study examined changes in wellbeing and work functioning among 278 employed autistic and/or ADHD adults (196 by 6-month follow-up) using the hybrid digital support tool Brain in Hand (BiH) over six months. Quantitative measures of wellbeing, productivity, perceived work-related energy, and coping were collected at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months, alongside qualitative free-text responses at 6 months. Significant improvements were observed across all quantitative outcomes over time. Improvements in wellbeing were associated with improvements in productivity; however, exploratory analyses suggested this relationship operated primarily through changes in perceived work-related energy. Reflexive thematic analysis identified five interrelated themes relating to experienced change, including increased self-awareness, proactive management of work demands, improved capacity to engage with work, and barriers related to workplace context and tool suitability. Participants described developing more sustainable patterns of work engagement through improved awareness, energy management, and workload regulation. Findings suggest that wellbeing and work functioning may be linked through proximal changes in day-to-day work capacity, particularly energy-related functioning. The study provides rare longitudinal evidence within an employed autistic and ADHD population and highlights the potential of hybrid digital support approaches within workplace contexts.

Lay Abstract
Many autistic and ADHD people struggle to stay in work. Stress, exhaustion, and workplaces not designed with their needs in mind can make it hard to feel well and perform effectively at the same time. 
This study followed 278 employed autistic and/or ADHD adults over six months, all using Brain in Hand (BiH) - a tool combining a support app with one-to-one coaching and on-demand human support. Participants completed surveys measuring wellbeing, productivity, energy, and coping at the start, three months, and six months, and described their experiences in their own words at six months.
Participants reported improvements across all four areas. Feeling energised at work more regularly appeared to be the key link between feeling better and working better. Participants described becoming more aware of their own needs, recognising overload earlier, and finding more sustainable ways of managing their workload. However, workplace flexibility and support shaped how much people benefited. These findings suggest that for many neurodivergent employees, supporting energy, self-awareness, and sustainable working patterns - not just wellbeing - may be critical for supporting people to thrive at work.

articles.title: Inteligencia Artificial y Aprendizaje: Una Agenda de Investigación desde la Ciencia Cognitiva
articles.authors: Jimmy Zambrano R.
articles.abstract:
La integración de la inteligencia artificial (IA) en la educación escolar plantea nuevas
oportunidades y desafíos. Cada vez es más común ver tutores inteligentes, asistentes de
estudio y plataformas adaptativas en las aulas. Sin embargo, este desarrollo está ocurriendo
mayormente al margen de nuestro más cualificado conocimiento sobre la cognición humana.
Por esto, es imperativo revisar el potencial impacto de la IA bajo la lente de cómo aprenden
las personas. Los numerosos estudios de la psicología educativa han identificado principios
fundamentales del aprendizaje humano, por ejemplo, la importancia de la carga cognitiva, la
metacognición y la personalización de la enseñanza, que deben guiar el desarrollo y adopción
de las tecnologías. Un uso acrítico de la IA podría sobrecargar la mente del estudiante o
fomentar hábitos superficiales. Por el contrario, un uso informado teóricamente podría
potenciar el aprendizaje y no solo la realización de tareas. Esta ponencia pretende examinar el
uso de la IA en el aprendizaje escolar con enfoque en tres aspectos clave: (1) su relación con
la carga cognitiva, (2) su impacto en la metacognición y (3) la personalización del entorno de
enseñanza mediada por IA. Además, pretende plantear una agenda de investigación desde
principios respaldados por la investigación sobre cómo ocurre el aprendizaje, a fin de
incorpora los hallazgos de la evidencia más robusta.

articles.title: Neurocognitive mechanisms of individual differences in cognition: a behavioral performance and EEG dataset
articles.authors: Mareike Hülsemann; Henrike Maria Jungeblut; Maximilian Lueckel; Benjamin Meyer; Anna-Lena Schubert
articles.abstract: The Mainz Network Study provides comprehensive behavioral and multimodal neural data from 215 healthy adults aged 18 to 40 years from the general population, supporting research on interindividual differences in cognitive ability. Across three sessions, participants completed the Berlin Intelligence Structure Test and Raven's Advanced Progressive Matrices (general and fluid intelligence), three binding tasks and three complex span tasks (working memory capacity), a cued task-switching paradigm (cognitive control), and three elementary cognitive tasks indexing processing speed (Posner letter matching, Sternberg memory scanning, two-choice reaction time). Electroencephalography (EEG) data and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data were collected. Here, we describe and publish the behavioral data (reaction times and accuracies) as well as EEG data recorded during cognitive control and processing speed tasks and during resting-state. Behavioral and EEG data are freely accessible via a RDC-ZPID repository. All data are BIDS-organized and shared raw.

articles.title: Identifying the visual and linguistic sources of reading efficiency:  A comparison of individual differences among deaf and hearing readers
articles.authors: Liz Schotter; Sara Milligan; Frances Cooley; Karen Emmorey
articles.abstract: Reading is a task that engages the vision-language interface, and the efficiency of this process may be related to how much of the text readers use to process visual and/or linguistic information during a fixation (i.e., their reading span size). Compared to typically hearing readers, deaf signers both read more efficiently (i.e., faster with equivalent comprehension) and have wider reading spans, suggesting a critical role for span size in reading efficiency. Using a linear regression analysis, we assessed the effects of two different span sizes (the word identification span, within which readers extract linguistic information, and the perceptual span, within which they extract the visuo-spatial layout), and reading comprehension ability, on reading rate for deaf signers (N = 50) and hearing non-signers (N = 109). For all readers, those with a larger word identification span read faster, but this relationship was weaker for the perceptual span, emphasizing the importance of parafoveal linguistic processing for reading speed. The effect of the word identification span was stronger for the deaf group, and only deaf signers showed a positive relationship between reading comprehension ability and reading rate (and word identification span size). These findings suggest that reading efficiency (exemplified by deaf signers) derives from an ability to extract linguistic information far from fixation in a way that supports both reading speed and comprehension.

articles.title: Mapping the neural dynamics of learning others' social preferences from decision timing
articles.authors: Sophie Bavard; Sebastian Gluth
articles.abstract: Inferring what others prefer from observing their behavior is a fundamental component of social cognition, shaping how we navigate everyday interactions. While research has shown that beyond decision outcome, humans also utilize decision speed as a reliable cue for social inference, the neural mechanisms underlying such inferences remain poorly understood. In this preregistered EEG and pupillometry study, we investigate how the brain extracts preference information from the temporal dynamics of others' decisions. Participants observed others making allocation decisions in a modified Dictator Game, seeing only how long each decision took and never which option was chosen, while we orthogonally manipulated decision speed and decision difficulty. EEG recordings revealed two distinct signatures: an early posterior positivity sensitive to the dictator's decision speed, consistent with temporal surprise, and a later fronto-central negativity sensitive to decision difficulty. Pupillometry converged with the fronto-central pattern: sensitivity to difficulty was present from the start, while sensitivity to speed emerged only after observers had learned to link speed with difficulty, pointing to a later belief-updating process. Together, these findings reveal that the brain integrates both temporal and decision-related information during social observation, going beyond simple duration tracking to support preference inference from others' decision speed.

articles.title: Pre-Structured Neural Architecture Enables Local Learning in Biological Systems
articles.authors: Ian S. Howard
articles.abstract: Backpropagation is widely regarded as the canonical solution to the credit assignment problem in deep learning. However, its debated biological plausibility has motivated decades of research into local learning rules. Here we argue that whether backpropagation is required for reliable credit assignment depends less on that debate than on the structural properties of the learning problem. We formulate credit assignment as a local inverse problem and argue that when architecture restricts learning to task-relevant subspaces that are locally identifiable, reasonably well-conditioned, and supplied with appropriate local error or modulatory signals, local plasticity can suffice; when architecture fails to impose such restrictions, some form of broader coordination may become necessary. We propose that neural architecture, shaped by evolution and development, pre-structures biological learning problems so that many such subspaces are already constrained in this way. We distinguish different regimes of biological learning, including one-shot episodic storage, gradual skill refinement, and structural representational adaptation. Within this framework, associative memory supports rapid storage and context-dependent retrieval, modular organization localizes credit assignment, and replay can improve conditioning and stabilize local refinement. Backpropagation, or some comparable form of broader coordination, is therefore not universally required for learning, but may become necessary when architecture and task structure fail to confine learning to identifiable, well-conditioned subspaces.

articles.title: The psychology of cringe
articles.authors: Paul Deutchman; Shaon Lahiri
articles.abstract: While cringe is an increasingly common term in social discourse and popular media—often used to describe a situation perceived as awkward or uncomfortable—we currently understand little about its underlying psychology and function in human social life. Across five studies (N = 1416; three preregistered), we examined the social cognition of cringe as a stable individual difference trait and as a fleeting affective state, investigating whether it is the same as, or functionally distinct from, vicarious embarrassment. We propose that cringe is a specific variety of vicarious embarrassment elicited by intentional, reputationally damaging social norm violations, that functions as a signal of normative competency—communicating recognition and alignment with group norms—and motivates norm compliance and enforcement. We first developed and validated the novel 20-item Cringe Scale to measure individual differences in trait cringe which we found positively predicted state cringe perceptions, incentivized donations, and behavioral intentions. Additionally, we found that cringe, as compared to vicarious embarrassment, was elicited more intensely by intentional and public norm violations, and was associated with heightened moral wrongness and punishment judgements as well as reduced empathy and prosocial motivation toward the transgressor. Path analyses revealed that perceptions of transgressor intent and norm violation severity mediated these differences. Together, our results establish cringe as a functionally distinct variety of vicarious embarrassment characterized by psychological distancing rather than empathic concern. The findings inform our understanding of an increasingly important self-conscious emotion and illuminate the adaptive social functions of cringe in social cognition.

articles.title: Does the Speaker Matter: EEG Evidence on the Influence of  Voice Information on Phoneme Learning in Infancy
articles.authors: Gisela Hanna Govaart; Katerina Chladkova; Antonio Schettino; Claudia Männel
articles.abstract: Infants acquire the sound categories of their native language within the first year of life, despite the lack of invariant acoustic features that define these categories across speakers and contexts. Given infants’ ability to process voice information and their preference for the familiar maternal voice, we investigated whether voice familiarity supports the acquisition of phonemes. Specifically, we tested whether infants learn a difficult native vowel contrast better from a familiar speaker compared to an unfamiliar speaker. During a 5-minute phoneme training, 71 3-month-old German-learning infants were exposed to a difficult native vowel contrast (/ɪ/–/ɛ/), spoken either by a previously familiarized speaker or by an unfamiliar speaker. In the subsequent test phase, measuring the electrophysiological mismatch response, we assessed infants’ ability to differentiate the vowels when spoken by either the training speaker or a novel speaker. Using Bayesian analysis, we found evidence against improved vowel discrimination after training with a familiar speaker. Instead, it was training with an unfamiliar speaker that resulted in facilitated vowel discrimination for a novel speaker at test. Our findings suggest that infants may not necessarily benefit from a familiar speaker during phoneme learning, particularly when the speaker is not a caregiver and thus lacks an emotional connection. Learning from an unfamiliar speaker, however, may enhance generalization across speakers, potentially due to increased attention driven by novelty.

articles.title: Voice-Familiarity Benefit in Infant Phoneme Perception: Acoustic versus Exemplar-based Familiarity
articles.authors: Gisela Hanna Govaart; Katerina Chladkova; Antonio Schettino; Claudia Männel
articles.abstract: Starting in utero, infants perceive voice information and preferentially attend to the maternal voice. In adults, speaker familiarity facilitates word and phoneme perception. The current study investigated whether a similar voice-familiarity benefit supports phoneme perception in early infancy, and whether this benefit is driven by acoustic familiarity with speaker voice, prior exposure to phoneme exemplars, or their combination. We tested 72 German-learning 3-month-olds, who were familiarized at home with two speakers. In the lab, infants received 5 minutes of training on a difficult native vowel contrast (/ɛ/–/ɪ/) from either one of the familiarized speakers or an unfamiliar speaker. Discrimination was tested with the electrophysiological mismatch response using three talkers: (1) the phoneme training speaker (familiarized or unfamiliar, depending on group), (2) the familiarized non-training speaker, and (3) a novel speaker (i.e., an unfamiliarized non-training speaker). This design dissociated acoustic familiarity (at-home familiarization) from phoneme-exemplar exposure (training tokens). Preregistered contrasts provided evidence against discrimination differences across test speakers when training was delivered by the unfamiliar speaker; this training might promote speaker-independent generalization, as predicted by abstractionist accounts of speech perception. By contrast, exploratory analyses revealed a voice-familiarity benefit in the group trained by the familiar speaker; this training may drive infants to rely more strongly on speaker-specific cues. Thus, the voice-familiarity benefit only arose when acoustic familiarity and exemplar-based familiarity were combined, suggesting that neither form alone was sufficient. Together, these results are consistent with hybrid accounts of speech perception in which exemplar-like and abstract representations coexist.

articles.title: The Musical Meaning Inventory: Development and Validation of a State-Based Measure of Musical Meanings
articles.authors: Connor Kirts; Tuomas Eerola
articles.abstract: Musical meanings are central to emotional experiences and music appreciation, but they also tend to be difficult to describe and organise. Particularly for empirical research, there is a need for a structured measure that would identify the likely broad areas of musical meaning that people consider to impact their specific situation and context. We developed a state-based measure of Musical Meaning Inventory (MMI) based initially on a music appreciation framework that was operationalised and evaluated in earlier studies. Here we tested the initially hypothesised three factors of Musical Meanings (Self, Source, and Structure) in a series of exploratory factor analyses (EFA). In this experiment, 180 participants rated items representing the factors within the context-specific vignettes. The EFA revealed a five-factor solution, which was in turn verified with a second EFA round with a new set of participants (N = 59). The emerging structure and the highest-performing items from the EFA rounds were used in a new experiment applying CFA (N = 100). The CFA results confirmed the existence of five factors within the musical meanings (structure, memory, identity, object, and culture). The psychometric properties of the inventory were excellent (high reliability, good to excellent discriminant and convergent validity, achieving strict measurement invariance) with 15 items in total. The new tool is well suited to research contexts where the dimensions of musical meaning require efficient, multidimensional assessment.

articles.title: Surpassing the Average Therapist: Performance of Commercial and Open-Source Large Language Models in Official Psychotherapist License Exams
articles.authors: Yannik Terhorst; Mareike Christina Hillebrand; Patricia Garatva; Jan-Patrick Schöller; Paul Hager; Andreas Triantafyllopoulos; Björn Schuller; Lasse Bosse Sander; Nicholas C. Jacobson; Michael V. Heinz
articles.abstract:
Background: Mental disorders are one of the leading causes of disease burden worldwide. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for improving mental healthcare. However, whether LLMs can match human therapists on key psychotherapeutic competencies in standardized assessments remains unclear. To inform this discussion, we applied the human psychotherapist qualification standard to LLMs and examined the performance of contemporary commercial and open-source LLMs against human performance in official state examinations.
Methods: In this pre-registered observational study, 14 widely used commercial (e.g., ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) and open-source (e.g., Llama-4-Maverick, DeepSeek-V3) LLMs were evaluated on three official psychotherapist state exams from Germany. Questions and official solutions were independently extracted from a secure training platform restricted to registered trainees. Models produced concise final answers via standardized prompts. Two blinded raters scored all responses against the official solutions. Performance was compared with pass thresholds and human cohort averages. Sensitivity analyses focused on domain-specific performance across 10 domains (i.e., psychological, medical, pharmacological, guideline, ICD, healthcare system and legal, behavioural therapy, psychodynamic therapy, and other therapies such as systemic).
Findings: All commercial models and 7 out of 11 open-source models exceeded the pass threshold on all exams. Five models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-o1-preview, Llama-4-Maverick, and DeepSeek-V3) surpassed the human cohort average across all exams, in some cases by more than one standard deviation. Domain-level analyses across all LLMs showed excellent performance in medical (M=95·3%, SD=9·58) and pharmacological (M=89·1%, SD=16·10) questions but substantial deficits in psychotherapy-related domains, including psychodynamic therapy (M=54·12%, SD=22·81) and healthcare system/legal knowledge (M=62·44%, SD=21·28).
Interpretation: Several commercial and leading open-source LLMs match or exceed average trainee performance on written qualification exams, even without specialized mental-health fine-tuning. These results highlight the potential of LLMs and identify promising commercial and open-source LLMs as candidates for future clinical applications. However, further research, clear qualification standards, safety benchmarks, crisis-response safeguards, and regulatory evaluation frameworks are needed before LLM-based systems can be responsibly integrated into mental healthcare.

articles.title: Theorising the arts as a health-promoting behaviour
articles.authors: Daisy Fancourt; Robert West; Andrew Steptoe; Sharifa Abdulla; Naoki Kondo; Pier Luigi Sacco; Agustin Ibanez
articles.abstract:
Arts engagement—defined as participation in or receptivity to creative practices (e.g. music, visual art, performance, storytelling, cultural activities)—is increasingly positioned within a health behaviour framework, though this remains under-theorised. This Review advances a conceptual argument for classifying arts engagement as a health-promoting behaviour, drawing on health behaviour theory, behavioural science, and public health. It situates this within the evolution of health behaviour models, from rational-choice frameworks (e.g. Health Belief Model) to biosocial, socio-ecological, dual-process, and complex systems approaches. Arts engagement is argued to meet ontological definitions of health-related and health-promoting behaviours (e.g. Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology), as it constitutes an individual behaviour capable of improving health outcomes, intentionally or incidentally.

Two critiques are addressed: (i) that framing arts engagement as a health behaviour risks instrumentalisation, and (ii) that it implies uniformly positive effects. The paper responds by conceptualising arts engagement along a continuum from informal, intrinsically motivated everyday practices to structured therapeutic interventions, analogous to other health-promoting behaviours (e.g. physical activity). It further examines mechanisms through which arts engagement functions as a health-promoting behaviour, drawing on experimental, quasi-experimental, and epidemiological evidence demonstrating associations and causal effects across psychological, cognitive, physiological, and social outcomes. Emphasis is placed on studies using advanced causal inference methods (e.g. propensity score matching, fixed effects, g-methods, instrumental variables) and longitudinal modelling (e.g. cross-lagged panel models) to disentangle independent and bidirectional relationships while accounting for confounding and selection bias.

A roadmap for policy, intervention, and research is outlined. This includes integrating arts engagement into behavioural surveillance, applying frameworks such as COM-B, and formalising classification within behaviour change ontologies. Future research directions include cross-cultural epidemiology, ecological momentary assessment, biomarker integration, exposome-informed models, and One Health frameworks to capture multi-level, life-course dynamics.

articles.title: A Few Psychometric Properties of Work-Family Boundaries Strength Scale: Results from Three Studies in Lithuania
articles.authors: Tadas Vadvilavicius; Aurelija Stelmokienė
articles.abstract: Valid and reliable research methods are needed to assess the work-home boundaries. Data from three cross-sectional studies were used to test the psychometric properties of the Lithuanian version of Hecht & Allen (2009) Work-Family Boundaries Strength scale (internal consistency, discriminant, convergent and structural validity). Results revealed that the Work-Family Boundaries Strength subscales had high internal consistency. Adequate convergent and discriminant validity was confirmed. Finally, structural validity was confirmed by exploratory graph analysis and confirmatory factor analysis, which revealed that the two-factor structure of the scale had the best fit. The results showed that the Work-Family Boundaries Strength scale is a reliable and valid tool for assessing work-family boundaries among Lithuanians.

articles.title: The Relationship Between Parenting Styles and Children’s Problem Behavior During the Transition to Preschool: The Moderating Role of Salivary Cortisol
articles.authors: Ke Shen; Mengru Xu; Wenxin Xu; zhixinyuan; Jingmei Wang; anonymous; Jinliang Qin
articles.abstract: The transition to preschool is a critical period for children’s behavioral adaptation, during which parenting styles may play an important role. Yet children may differ in their sensitivity to caregiving environments depending on their physiological stress regulation. This study examined whether salivary cortisol moderated the associations between parenting styles and internalizing and externalizing problems during the transition to preschool. Participants were 68 Chinese children aged 2.5 to 4 years. Caregivers reported parenting styles and children’s behavioral problems, and children’s diurnal cortisol levels were derived from multi-day saliva sampling. Results showed that cortisol moderated the associations of authoritarian and authoritative parenting, but not permissive parenting, with children’s problem behaviors. Authoritarian parenting was associated with more internalizing and externalizing problems among children with higher cortisol levels. Authoritative parenting was associated with fewer internalizing problems among children with high cortisol levels but more internalizing problems among those with low cortisol levels. Permissive parenting was consistently associated with more internalizing and externalizing problems regardless of cortisol levels. These findings suggest that biological sensitivity to context during the transition to preschool is both parenting style-specific and outcome-specific. They also highlight the need to move beyond uniform recommendations about “ideal parenting” and toward more individualized parenting support that considers children’s physiological stress regulation and regulatory needs.

articles.title: Investigating the prospective associations of dietary risk factors with the risk of incident depression and anxiety in adulthood: Evidence from the UK Biobank
articles.authors: Rebecca Orr; Mojtaba Lotfaliany; Felice Jacka; Elizabeth Gamage; Melissa Lane; Emma Todd; Deb Junyi Zhang; Adrienne O’ Neil; Deborah Ashtree
articles.abstract:
Background: Mental disorders are leading contributors to disease burden worldwide. Nutritional psychiatry research supports an important role for diet in the prevention and management of mental disorders, but the relevance of specific dietary exposures requires further investigation. We examined associations of Global Burden of Disease-defined food groups and nutrients with subsequent depression and anxiety outcomes in UK Biobank participants, following the Global Burden of Disease Lifestyle And mental Disorders Taskforce protocol. 
Methods: We used data from the UK Biobank, a large biomedical cohort of more than 500,000 UK participants. Dietary intake was assessed using repeated 24-hour dietary recalls collected between 2011 and 2012 and classified according to Global Burden of Disease dietary exposure definitions. Depression and anxiety outcomes were assessed using the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 and Generalised Anxiety Disorder-7 in the 2016 Mental Wellbeing Questionnaire, after excluding participants with pre-existing depression or anxiety from the respective models. Associations were estimated using complete-case log-binomial regression models with robust standard errors, adjusting for demographic, socioeconomic, and lifestyle covariates. 
Results: Analyses included 46,503 participants in depression models and 48,656 participants in anxiety models. Overall, dietary intake was generally suboptimal, with many participants not meeting theoretical minimum risk exposure levels across several dietary factors. In fully adjusted models, higher intakes of wholegrains and fibre were associated with lower depression risk, whereas higher intakes of sugar-sweetened beverages and sodium were associated with higher depression risk. For anxiety, higher intakes of sugar-sweetened beverages, sodium and legumes were associated with higher risk, although the legume association was attenuated in post-hoc analyses excluding baked beans. Higher intakes of red meat, processed meat and trans fatty acids were associated with lower anxiety risk; however, the trans fatty acid association was attenuated in post-hoc analyses. 
Conclusions: In this large UK Biobank cohort, selected dietary exposures were associated with subsequent depression and anxiety outcomes. Findings were most consistent for wholegrains, fibre, sugar-sweetened beverages, and sodium, suggesting that specific aspects of diet quality may be relevant to later mental health. Associations for other dietary exposures were less consistent across outcomes, adjustment sets, and subgroup analyses. Further research is needed to confirm these findings and clarify potential mechanisms.

articles.title: Understanding Work–Family Enrichment Through a Cultural Lens: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Tadas Vadvilavicius; Aurelija Stelmokienė
articles.abstract: The aim of this scoping review is to synthesize the existing research about the relationship between work-family enrichment and cultural dimensions. Four databases were used to identify relevant articles. An additional strategy of Google Scholar to search for relevant studies was performed. In all, nine articles were identified. The scoping review suggests that only three cultural dimensions are being analyzed: gender traditionalism, humane orientation, and individualism/collectivism. Furthermore, the review confirms that culture has an impact on both work-family enrichment and/or how enrichment is related to other variables in a different cultural context. Theoretical explanations of the relationships are provided. Results are discussed, and ideas for future research are proposed.

articles.title: Exploring whether the Conscioussness mediate the relation between the Presentation of False Self on SNS and Narcissism
articles.authors: Phd, Tatjana Slavko Mentus Kandić; Irena Stojković; Božidar Filipović; Marijana Veselinović
articles.abstract:
Narcissism has been consistently linked to self-regulatory processes involving impression management
and self-enhancement. Therefore we explore the prediction of false self-presentation on social network sites
